entry.title: How to Configure and Apply Custom Themes Using UI Theme Designer in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud via BTP
entry.content:
Overview:

This guide provides a clear, step-by-step walkthrough on how to create and apply a custom UI theme for your SAP Fiori Launchpad using the UI Theme Designer in SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It covers the full configuration process—from setting up services and assigning roles, to designing a theme in SAP Build Work Zone and integrating it with your SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system. By the end, you'll be able to deliver a consistent, branded user experience across your SAP applications.

Step 1: Configuration in Btp cockpit

  • In Btp cockpit go to the subaccount.
  • Go to services à service marketplace and under application development and automation you will have UI theme designer.
  • Click on UI theme designer and create new instance.
  • Select the space and give the Instance name and create it.

 

  • Then Assign the UI theme designer specific roles for user.
  • Goto security, role collections and create new collection for UI theme designer.
  • Create and assign the CustomCssEditor, Editor, Publisher, Viewer roles to the collection.
  • Now assign the user Id to the role collection.

  • To Create a new custom theme design, we need a site and that can be used from the SAP Build Work Zone.
  • Now Go to services à service marketplace and under application development and automation you will find SAP Build Work Zone.
  • Click on it and create by choosing the plan standard under the subscriptions.
  • Click on create and the application will be created.
  • Now go to the instance and subscriptions and will find the applications and instances created.

  • Now assign roles to the user for workzone.
  • Go to security à user and click on the user and assign the specific role collection to it.
  • Assign the roles Launchpad_Admin, Launchpad_Advanced_Theming and Launchpad_External_User to user.

Step 2: Create new UI theme

  • Go to the instance and subscription tab and click on the SAP Build Work Zone.
  • In the workzone, create an site to work on the designer.
  • Enter the site name and create it.

  • Now open the created site and click on the user profile in the top right.

  • In that click on the theme designer.
  • In that you will find the list of themes available and at the bottom will be an option to open the launch theme designer. Click and open it.

  • UI theme designer will open and click on create a new theme at top to create new.

  • Now select a theme that you want to customize and click on create theme.

  • Then after customizing the changes and click on the theme on left top and click on save & publish.

  • After that it will ask the theme id and save it and publish it.

  • After that the theme will be available in the site. You can verify it by opening the theme designer in the site.

Step 3 : Add theme in the public cloud system

  • Go to the instance and subscription in the btp subaccount.
  • You will find the UI theme designer instance, click on it and create the service key.

 

  • Enter the service key name and click on create.

  • Then click on the service key and copy the Credentials in the json format.

  • Now open the public cloud system that you want to add the theme.
  • In the system open the communication arrangements app.
  • Click on create to create a new communication arrangement.
  • Use the scenario id as SAP_COM_0623 as this is for the UI theme designer integration.
  • And now paste the copied service key credentials and click on create.

  • Now edit the created arrangement and add the theme id in the Additional theme ids and save.

  • Open the Manage Content Security Policy app and go to trusted sites and open the UI_RESOURSES_FONTS and just enable the options for managed by customers by clicking on add.

  • Then click on the user icon and go to settings, in the appearance tab you can find the added custom theme.

  • Click on it and save it and verify the theme.

  • The theme is applied successfully.
  • When some changes is done in the designer, save and publish it in designer and in the system refresh the communication arrangement by saving it.

Conclusion:

Customizing the SAP Fiori Launchpad with your own UI theme is a powerful way to align your enterprise applications with your organization's branding and improve the overall user experience. By following this step-by-step guide, you've learned how to configure the UI Theme Designer in SAP BTP, create and manage themes using SAP Build Work Zone, and successfully integrate those themes into your SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system. With the theme applied, your users can enjoy a consistent, visually appealing interface. For any future changes, simply update and publish the theme in the designer, then refresh the communication arrangement in the system to reflect the updates instantly.

entry.title: Build, Configure, and Deploy SAP CAP Projects to the Cloud
entry.content:
🚀A Comprehensive, Step-by-Step Guide to Creating and Deploying a Full CAP Application with SAP Fiori UI in SAP Business Application Studio

The Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model provided by SAP is a powerful, opinionated framework designed to streamline the process of building enterprise-grade services and applications that are scalable, extensible, and cloud-ready. In this extensive guide, we will delve deeply into each and every step required to build a full-fledged CAP project from scratch using SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), enhance it with a Fiori UI, and deploy the entire application stack to the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) on Cloud Foundry. This guide is structured with elaborate explanations for beginners and intermediate developers alike.

Prerequisites and Initial Setup

Before we begin, ensure you have a valid SAP BTP trial or productive account with appropriate entitlements, particularly for SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Build Work Zone, which are essential for running managed application routers and deploying HTML5 applications. You also need access to SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), which will serve as your cloud-based IDE for this project.

== 1. Creating a Dev Space in SAP Business Application Studio ==

  1. Open your browser and navigate to the SAP Business Application Studio.

  2. On the landing page, click on the "Create Dev Space" button, which will prompt you to select a template.

  3. From the available options, choose "Full-Stack Cloud Application" as it comes pre-configured with essential tools for CAP and Fiori development.

  4. Assign a recognizable name to your dev space, for example, cap-bookshop, to easily identify it later.

  5. Check the box labeled "SAP HANA Tools" to enable support for creating and managing HANA database artifacts.

  6. Click on "Create Dev Space" and wait patiently for the status of the space to change to "RUNNING."

  7. Once the dev space is up and running, click on it to launch the integrated development environment where you will build your application.

== 2. Provisioning a SAP HANA Cloud Instance ==

  1. Log in to the SAP BTP Cockpit using your credentials.

  2. Navigate into your global account and select the appropriate subaccount.

  3. Under the navigation pane, click on "Service Marketplace" to view the available services.

  4. Search for "SAP HANA Cloud" and click on it when it appears in the search results.

  5. Click on the "Create" button to begin provisioning a new HANA database instance.

  6. Choose the SAP HANA Database (ensure you do not select SAP HANA Cloud Tools).

  7. Sign-In with cloud foundry account and select your space from the dropdown
  8. Enter a unique name for the instance (e.g., hana), specify your required configurations, set an admin password.

  9. Administrator Section:

    • Administrator Username: Auto-filled as DBADMIN.

    • Administrator Password: Enter a secure password.

    • Confirm Password: Retype to confirm.
      🔐Keep in mind: Passwords for DBADMIN expire every 180 days, so set a reminder to change them before expiry.

  11. Configure SAP HANA Database Settings

    In this step, you'll define your database instance's technical specifications.

     

    • Available Memory Range:
      In the trial version, it's fixed at 16 GB.

    ℹ️Note: Trial accounts come with fixed configurations for memory and storage. For more flexibility, a commercial account is required.

  12. Network & Cloud Connector Configuration

    In this step, configure the network access and optionally enable the Cloud Connector if needed.

    Allow all IP addresses (Selected in this guide)
    Suitable for trial and development — easiest for testing external tools.
  13. Click "Create" in final stage

  14. The provisioning process may take a few minutes to complete. Once done, your database will be ready for binding.

  15. (Important) Whenever trying to deploy or bind your service or application ensure HANA Cloud is Started and running

== 3. Creating a New CAP Project Using Terminal Commands ==

  1. Inside your running BAS dev space, open a new terminal window by navigating to the menu: Terminal → New Terminal.

  2. Change the current directory to your desired workspace, typically located at /home/user/projects/:

cd /home/user/projects/

  1. Run the CAP project initialization command:

cds init bookshop
cd bookshop
 

  1. Your project will now have a structured directory layout containing essential folders such as db for data models, for services, app for UI modules (currently empty), and package.json which manages dependencies.

== 4. Modeling Data and Defining Services ==

4.1 Define the Data Model

Create a new CDS file in the db folder, typically named data-model.cds:

namespace my.bookshop;

entity Books {
  key ID    : Integer;
      title : String;
      author: String;
      stock : Integer;
}

4.2 Add Sample Data in CSV Format

Create a new CSV file inside the db/data directory named my.bookshop-Books.csv:

ID;title;author;stock
1;The Hobbit;J.R.R. Tolkien;15
2;Harry Potter;J.K. Rowling;30
3;The Alchemist;Paulo Coelho;20

4.3 Define a CAP Service

Inside the srv folder, create a service definition file named catalog-service.cds:

using my.bookshop from '../db/data-model';

service CatalogService {
  entity Books as projection on my.bookshop.Books;
}

== 5. Configuring for SAP HANA Deployment ==

CAP defaults to SQLite for local development, but to deploy on SAP BTP, you must configure it to use SAP HANA.

  1. Add HANA configuration for production:

cds add hana --for production

  1. Add hybrid setup (for both dev and prod compatibility):

cds add hana --for hybrid

  1. Generate a Multi-Target Application (MTA) descriptor:

cds add mta

  1. Install all necessary project dependencies:

npm install

== 6. Previewing Your CAP App Locally ==

To see how your app behaves locally, start the development server:

cds watch

The terminal will display a URL where your CAP service is accessible. Click the link to open a preview interface, which features a basic UI generated using Fiori Elements.

== 7. Adding a Managed Application Router ==

  1. Right-click on the mta.yaml file and select Create MTA Module from Template.

  2. Choose the Approuter Configuration template.

  3. Follow the wizard:

    • Use a Managed Approuter.

    • Name it samplerouter or another descriptive identifier.

    • Indicate that a Fiori UI will be added later.

  4. Finish the wizard to generate the approuter configuration, including a xs-security.json file.

== 8. Adding XSUAA for Authentication and Authorization ==

XSUAA is required for deploying CAP apps to Cloud Foundry with authentication support.

cds add xsuaa --for production
npm install

This updates your package.json and adds authentication requirements under the "requires" section.

== 9. Creating a SAP Fiori UI App ==

  1. Right-click mta.yaml → Create MTA Module from Template.

  2. Choose SAP Fiori Application and select List Report Page as the template.

  3. In the Data Source step:

    • Select Local CAP Project

    • Pick the bookshop project

    • Choose the CatalogService (Node.js)

  4. Confirm Books as the main entity.

  5. Set module name (e.g., booksui), title, and namespace.

  6. Enable deployment options and FLP configuration.

  7. For deployment:

  8. Target: Cloud Foundry

  9.  

    • Destination: Local CAP Project API

    • Semantic Object: bookshop

    • Action: show

    • Title: Books

Finish the wizard to generate the UI module.

== 10. Deploying the Application to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry ==

10.1 Login to Cloud Foundry

Use the command palette:

CTRL + Shift + P → CF: Login to Cloud Foundry

Provide the API endpoint, credentials, and select the correct organization and space.

10.2 Bind the HANA HDI Container

  1. Open the SAP HANA Projects panel in BAS.

          Bind to a HDI Container

         Select "+ Create a new service instance" 

  2. Click bookshop-db → Bind → Create new service instance.

  3. Name it bookshop-db and enable auto undeploy.

10.3 Build and Deploy the MTA Archive

 

Run the following in your terminal:

mbt build
cf deploy mta_archives/bookshop_1.0.0.mtar

This creates and deploys your application archive.

== 11. Verifying Deployment and Launching Your Fiori App ==

  1. In the BTP cockpit, go to Services → Instances. You should see instances for the db, approuter, CAP service, and xsuaa.

  2. Go to HTML5 Applications (enabled via SAP Build Work Zone) and find your booksui app.

  3. Launch the app to see the same Fiori UI you previewed locally, now hosted on the cloud.

entry.title: Discrete, Process, or Repetitive? Understanding the Manufacturing Options (For Beginners!) 🏭
entry.content:
SAP Production Planning (PP): Discrete, Process, or Repetitive? Understanding Your Manufacturing Options

Welcome to the world of SAP Production Planning (PP)! If you're new to SAP, or just looking to understand how businesses manage their manufacturing, one of the first things you'll encounter is the concept of different production types. SAP PP offers flexible approaches to cater to diverse manufacturing scenarios.

Choosing the right type in SAP is crucial because it dictates how materials are managed, how production orders are structured, and how costs are tracked. Let's break down the three primary manufacturing options in SAP PP for beginners.

1. Discrete Manufacturing: Building Unique Products, Piece by Piece

Imagine a factory assembling cars, building complex electronics, or crafting custom furniture. These are perfect examples of Discrete Manufacturing.

What defines it?

  • Individual Products: You produce distinct, identifiable products, often with serial numbers or batch numbers. Each finished good is a unique item, even if it's part of a larger series.
  • Production Orders: Production is typically driven by specific production orders, where each order is created for a defined quantity of a particular product.
  • Flexible Production Lines: Production lines and processes can be reconfigured frequently to adapt to different product models or customer specifications.
  • Material Issuance: Materials are issued specifically to individual production orders, ensuring precise cost tracking for each manufactured item.
  • Bill of Materials (BOM) & Routing: These are heavily used to define what components go into a product and how it's manufactured (the sequence of operations).

When to use it: When your products are distinct, often complex, and require detailed tracking of each assembly step and component consumption.

2. Process Manufacturing: Mixing, Blending, and Transforming

Now, shift your focus to industries that create products by mixing, blending, or reacting ingredients. Think about chemicals, pharmaceuticals, food, beverages, or even paints. This is the realm of Process Manufacturing.

What defines it?

  • Batch-Oriented Production: Products are typically manufactured in batches. Ingredients are combined, processed, and transformed into a final product or an intermediate.
  • Recipes/Formulas (not BOMs): Instead of a Bill of Materials, Process Manufacturing uses "Recipes" or "Formulas" to define the quantities of ingredients and the sequence of processing steps.
  • Liquid, Powder, Gas Handling: This type often involves managing bulk materials like liquids, powders, or gases that are difficult to count as discrete units.
  • Resource Management Focus: There's a strong emphasis on managing resources like tanks, reactors, and pipelines, as well as tracking by-products or co-products that may result from the process.
  • Process Orders: Production is executed using "Process Orders" which are tailored to the specific batch or formula being produced.

When to use it: When your products are created through a series of transformations, mixing, or chemical reactions, and often involve batch-specific characteristics.

3. Repetitive Manufacturing (REM): High Volume, Consistent Flow

Imagine an automotive assembly line churning out thousands of identical car parts, or a factory producing vast quantities of standardized consumer goods like batteries or light bulbs. This is where Repetitive Manufacturing (REM) shines.

What defines it?

  • High Volume & Standardization: REM is designed for continuous, high-volume production of the same, standardized products over long periods.
  • Quantity and Period-Based: Instead of individual production orders, production is planned and executed based on quantities per period (e.g., "produce 500 units of X per day/week").
  • Production Lines (not orders): The focus is on the production line itself, and materials are typically issued to the line, not to specific, individual orders.
  • Streamlined Processes: It aims for simplicity and efficiency, reducing the need for detailed order-specific tracking once the line is set up.
  • Backflushing: Materials consumption and activity confirmations are often "backflushed" (automatically posted) at the end of the production cycle or when a certain quantity is completed, further streamlining the process.

When to use it: When you have a stable product design, a high and consistent demand, and production occurs on dedicated, repetitive lines.

Choosing the Right Path in SAP PP

Selecting the correct manufacturing type in SAP PP is a foundational decision. It directly impacts how your master data (materials, BOMs, routings, recipes), planning processes (MRP), and execution steps (production/process orders, confirmations) are configured and used.

Understanding these distinctions is your first step to mastering SAP Production Planning. Each type is optimized to handle different business realities, ensuring that SAP accurately reflects and supports your manufacturing operations.

entry.title: New SAP Design System Portal for SAP Fiori Design Guidelines, and New UI Kits for Designers
entry.content:
Good news for creators! The SAP Fiori design guidelines site is now the SAP Design System portal. The new portal is your one-stop-shop to find everything about the design system. And that’s not all: we have released comprehensive new UI Kits for designers in Figma, the tool used by designers around the world.

 

SAP Design System Portal

The new SAP Design System portal brings together the product design guidelines SAP Fiori for web, SAP Fiori for iOS, and SAP Fiori for Android, as well as to the new digital design system guidelines. Bringing these all together is not the only improvement we’ve made, we have also:

  • Enhanced usability: easier to navigate and find what you need.
  • Improved performance: much faster page load times.
  • Simplified versioning: the 50+ guideline versions of the past for SAP Fiori for web have now been consolidated into six versions, corresponding to the six versions of SAPUI5 which are in long-term maintenance.
  • Product specific content: in addition to content which is applicable everywhere, we now also make it clear when content applies only to specific products, for example due to dependencies to product-specific technologies. At the moment we have one such section in the portal, for SAP S/4HANA (which also covers SAP Cloud ERP and SAP Cloud ERP Private).
  • Feedback button: you can now give us direct feedback on the content, via the feedback button available on the bottom right of each page (as you can see in Figure 1).

SAP’s digital design system reflects our evolved brand and is aligned to our product design system. It serves as a reference for all who contribute content to our public-facing web sites.

Figure 1: The SAP Design System portal start page. Alt Text: Image with header saying “SAP Design System”, with a “See what’s new” button below it, and then four subsections in a horizontal row: SAP Fiori for Web, SAP Fiori for Android, SAP Fiori for iOS and SAP’s Digitial Design System. A “Feedback” button is on the bottom right.

Have a look at this 45 second video to get an overview:

 

 

UI Kits for Designers

The new UI Kits available for designers in Figma make it easy for designers to design accessible UIs at enterprise scale, for web applications, mobile iOS and Android applications as well as wearables. The UI Kits provide reusable components for web and mobile, along with web floorplans, which give you what you need out-of-the-box.

In addition, we give you design resources for designing for accessibility and for performance.

Figure 2: SAP’s UI Kits for Designers in Figma. Alt-Text: The image shows two rows of three cards representing UI Kits: SAP Fiori for Android, SAP Fiori for Web, Designing for Performance, SAP Fiori for iOS, SAP Fiori for watchOS, and SAP Fiori for Wear OS. On the very left of the image information about SAP is listed, with links to resources such as www.sap.com/design-system.

Get an overview and see how easy it is for designers to put together a new UI in this 43 second video:

 

 

User Experience in SAP Community

I’m looking forward to seeing how you like the new SAP Design System portal and the new UI Kits for Designers. Do use the SAP Community to exchange your ideas and best practices by using the “User Experience” tag.

Here is a link to the latest posts for User Experience.

For an overview of design at SAP, check out www.sap.com/design.

entry.title: SAP User Experience in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502
entry.content:
January 24 marked the general availability of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 release. As a cornerstone of modern enterprise, SAP Business Suite empowers organizations to scale, innovate, and adapt faster than ever. SAP cloud ERP applications, SAP Business AI, and SAP Business Data Cloud come together to deliver exceptional business value. But even the most robust cloud ERP wouldn't reach its full potential without a smooth user experience. That’s why we’re committed to evolving our product, listening to your feedback, and pushing the boundaries to deliver a user experience that matches the pace of today's dynamic work environment. Let me show you just how we achieve this in this release. Honorary mention: Generative AI is giving main character energy in this release.

Check out my video below to get a first glimpse into what’s coming in the area of SAP User Experience in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502:

 

Joule, the AI Copilot

Following a successful early adoption phase, Joule is generally available and fully integrated in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. This AI copilot utilizes generative AI to help users complete tasks more efficiently by using natural language to express their business needs. It offers quick navigation to applications, instant insights into key business data like purchase orders, and concise user enablement content to speed up tasks. Joule also simplifies personalization of the My Home section, allowing users to create cards with critical data for immediate insights. Its capabilities include recommending relevant applications, providing insightful information on business situations without needing to open the application, and offering brief summaries of help content. Users can directly create and add preview cards to the Insights section of My Home.

 

 

AI-Assisted Smart Summarization

AI-assisted smart summarization allows you to summarize object pages of SAP Fiori elements-based applications with the power of generative AI. The summarization can be reviewed and modified in a rich text editor and easily copied into productivity tools, such as email or chat, for follow-ups with co-workers and customers. The tool also facilitates efficient sharing through seamless copy-to-clipboard functionality for quick follow-ups. Additionally, users have the option to provide feedback on their experience with the feature, contributing to its continual improvement and adaptation to user needs.

 

 

AI-Assisted Easy Filter

The AI-assisted easy filter leverages state-of-the-art generative AI to redefine how users approach filtering within SAP Fiori elements-based list reports. Rather than setting filter values manually through type-ahead inputs and value helps, you can simply type your search query into the filter field. This approach not only enhances the user experience but also provides easy access to recent and favorite filter queries. The AI results are reliable, offering an intuitive and click-efficient experience, which is particularly beneficial for new users navigating the system.

 

 

Currently in Beta Testing: AI-Assisted Easy Enterprise Search

The AI-assisted easy enterprise search leverages generative AI to enable natural language querying for business objects throughout the solution. By simply typing your search query into the search field of the SAP Fiori launchpad, users can quickly access the information they need. This feature is especially valuable for its time-saving capabilities when searching with known criteria, providing reliable AI results and offering an intuitive, click-efficient experience that benefits new users in particular.

Register here to start testing: Newest AI-assisted user experience features in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

 

Currently in Beta Testing: AI-Assisted Smart Personalization of My Home

The AI-assisted smart personalization of My Home allows you to use natural language through the power of generative AI to add insights cards to your My Home experience. This feature streamlines the process by enabling you to add insights cards directly from My Home with just two clicks, including a preview step for the end-user to review the AI-generated result. This tool offers several benefits, such as saving time when adding insights cards, providing better insights into business data, and delivering reliable AI results. It is designed to be intuitive and click-efficient, making it especially valuable for new users.

Register here to start testing: Newest AI-assisted user experience features in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

 

Currently in Beta Testing: AI-Assisted Error Explanation

AI-assisted error explanation supports in understanding errors in SAP Fiori elements-based applications better and provides first recommendations on how to solve the errors by leveraging the power of generative AI. This innovative feature is seamlessly integrated into the current error visualization, enabling users to generate explanations on demand, which significantly saves time and improves workflow efficiency. By offering intuitive navigation, it delivers both the explanatory insight and recommended solutions within a single, click-efficient action. The process includes well-structured results that clarify the nature of the error and suggest practical resolutions.

Register here to start testing: Newest AI-assisted user experience features in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

 

Interested in all things AI? Read this blog posts by my colleagues Yannick Peterschmitt and Sang Dao:

  • Artificial Intelligence and Other Intelligent ERP Use Cases in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502
  • Artificial Intelligence & Technologies in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502

 

To-Dos in Microsoft Teams Integration

The integration of To-Dos in Microsoft Teams allows users to seamlessly access their workflow items from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition directly through the SAP S/4HANA Cloud for Microsoft Teams app. When you receive a workflow item in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, it triggers a notification in Microsoft Teams. This integration enables you to open the My Inbox application from Microsoft Teams to view details of the workflow items and to take immediate action. It also supports mobile approvals, ensuring flexibility in managing workflows on the go.

 

Smart Heads-Up of Situations in To Dos

Smart heads-up in To Dos ensure that you're always up to speed on situations requiring your attention, allowing you to manage all critical matters efficiently. This feature provides immediate insight and actionability through an intuitive interface, ensuring that nothing important slip through the cracks. By staying informed about and addressing potential issues early on, it significantly boosts productivity. Additionally, it offers support in prioritizing actions effectively. Users can get an overview of situations on both the standard and extended frameworks directly in the To-Dos section. With just one click, they can access detailed information by navigating to the My Situations and My Situations – Extended apps, depending on their needs.

 

As you can see, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 not only promises a transformed user experience but also enhances organizational productivity and strategic decision-making, bringing you closer to achieving seamless operational excellence, rooted in innovative technology.

Find out more about SAP User Experience in the numerous blog posts by my colleagues Thomas Reiss and Conrad Bernal.

The next release is just around the corner! See you in a bit. 😊

_____________________

The Future of Business, Today: Get Your Access to AI-Assisted Features Thanks to this Limited Promotion Now! 

The SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 update is a strategic investment that combines advanced AI with powerful enterprise management tools. Whether scaling your business, enhancing productivity, or building sustainable operations, its capabilities help support a more agile and resilient future.

Sign up for following features through the limited Promotion for AI-assisted Features in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (for all countries, customers, and partners):

  • The easy filter and smart summarization features in SAP Fiori allow for efficient, natural language filtering of business data and seamless summarization capabilities, enabling users to quickly extract relevant information and jump-start subsequent collaboration. 
  • Facilitate easier understanding and interpretation of financial data with insights from cost center reviews and explanations of fixed-asset depreciation keys.

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts.

Feel free to follow me on SAP Community and on LinkedIn to not miss any updates.

📽️ Watch the Replays of Our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 Early Release Series!

From January 21 to January 24, we hosted a series of 21 compelling live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find a 43-minute-long recording and a presentation for SAP User Experience as well as 20 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand–anywhere, anytime.

🔎Find All Our Assets

GROW with SAP is a comprehensive package designed to help businesses of all sizes to successfully adopt cloud ERP, anchored by SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for optimized processes and intelligent, sustainable operations. We at Cloud ERP Product Success and Cloud Co-Innovation offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: Developing a UI5 App with Value Help from CSV, Charts with VizFrame, and Excel Upload/Download
entry.content:
SAPUI5 (SAP User Interface for HTML5) is a JavaScript-based UI framework developed by SAP. It is used for building responsive, cross-platform web applications that run in desktop and mobile browsers. SAPUI5 is the foundation of SAP Fiori, which is SAP’s design language and user experience strategy.

Key Features:

  • MVC Architecture: Supports the Model-View-Controller pattern for clean code separation.

  • Rich UI Controls: Includes a comprehensive set of UI controls optimized for business applications.

  • Data Binding: Supports multiple data models (JSON, XML, OData) for dynamic content rendering.

  • Responsive Design: Adapts to different screen sizes and devices automatically.

  • Theming and Customization: Allows theming via the SAP Theme Designer.

 

What is BAS (Business Application Studio)?

SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) is a modern development environment provided by SAP. It's designed for building business applications, especially those based on SAP technologies like SAPUI5, SAP Fiori, CAP (Cloud Application Programming model), and more.

Think of BAS as SAP's version of Visual Studio Code — but hosted in the cloud and optimized for enterprise application development.

A Dev Space in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) is a pre-configured, isolated development environment tailored for specific types of SAP development — like SAPUI5, CAP, or Fiori.

 

What this app does – simplified scenarios:

  • Show a page titled "Dummy App" with a clean, centered header.

  • Collect user input using a form with the following fields:

    • Name input field with a value help dialog (e.g., a search or selection help).

    • Age input field, also with value help.

    • Company input field, again with value help.

  • Let users submit the form by clicking the "Submit" button, which likely stores or processes the entered data.

  • Display a small bar chart:

    • Shows the number of entries (Count) grouped by Age.

    • Useful for visualizing how many users fall into each age group.

  • Provide a results table that displays the submitted data:

    • Each row shows a person's Name, Age, and Company.

    • Table grows dynamically to show more entries as needed.

  • Allow data export and import:

    • "Download" button lets users export the table data (likely to Excel).

    • "Upload" button lets users import data from a file (probably Excel or CSV).

 

View1.view.xml :

<mvc:View
    controllerName="com.sap.btp.project1.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:viz="sap.viz.ui5.controls"
    xmlns:viz.data="sap.viz.ui5.data"
    xmlns:viz.feeds="sap.viz.ui5.controls.common.feeds"
    xmlns:export="sap.ui.export">

    <!-- Wrap Page inside App to enable scrolling properly -->
    <App>
        <Page id="page" showHeader="false">
            <ScrollContainer height="100%" vertical="true" focusable="true">
                <VBox class="sapUiMediumMargin">

                    <!-- App Header -->
                    <VBox class="appHeader sapUiMediumMargin" alignItems="Center">
                        <Text text="Dummy App" textAlign="Center" class="appTitle" />
                    </VBox>

                    <!-- Main Layout -->
                    <HBox wrap="Wrap">

                        <!-- Form with Value Help Inputs -->
<VBox class="formContainer sapUiSmallMarginEnd">

    <!-- Name Input with Value Help -->
<HBox class="sapUiTinyMarginBottom" alignItems="Center">
    <Label text="Name :" width="100px"/>
    <Input id="nameInput"
           width="300px"
           showValueHelp="true"
           value="{/name}"
           valueHelpRequest="onNameValueHelp" />
</HBox>


   <!-- Age -->
<HBox class="sapUiTinyMarginBottom" alignItems="Center">
    <Label text="Age :" width="100px"/>
    <Input id="ageInput"
           width="300px"
           showValueHelp="true"
           value="{/age}"
           valueHelpRequest="onAgeValueHelp" />
</HBox>

<!-- Company -->
<HBox class="sapUiTinyMarginBottom" alignItems="Center">
    <Label text="Company :" width="100px"/>
    <Input id="companyInput"
           width="300px"
           showValueHelp="true"
           value="{/company}"
           valueHelpRequest="onCompanyValueHelp" />
</HBox>


    <Button text="Submit" press="onSubmit" type="Emphasized" class="sapUiSmallMarginTop" />
</VBox>


                        <!-- Smaller Chart -->
                        <VBox>
                            <viz:VizFrame id="ageChart"
                                          height="250px"
                                          width="400px"
                                          uiConfig="{applicationSet:'fiori'}"
                                          vizType="column">
                                <viz:dataset>
                                    <viz.data:FlattenedDataset data="{/ageChartData}">
                                        <viz.data:dimensions>
                                            <viz.data:DimensionDefinition name="Age" value="{age}" />
                                        </viz.data:dimensions>
                                        <viz.data:measures>
                                            <viz.data:MeasureDefinition name="Count" value="{count}" />
                                        </viz.data:measures>
                                    </viz.data:FlattenedDataset>
                                </viz:dataset>
                                <viz:feeds>
                                    <viz.feeds:FeedItem uid="valueAxis" type="Measure" values="Count"/>
                                    <viz.feeds:FeedItem uid="categoryAxis" type="Dimension" values="Age"/>
                                </viz:feeds>
                            </viz:VizFrame>
                        </VBox>
                    </HBox>

                    <!-- Table & Buttons -->
                    <VBox class="sapUiLargeMarginTop sapUiResponsiveMargin">
                        <HBox justifyContent="End">
                            <Button text="Download"
                                    press="onDownloadExcel"
                                    type="Emphasized"
                                    class="sapUiMediumMarginTop sapUiSmallMarginEnd"/>

                            <Button text="Upload"
                                    press="onUploadFile"
                                    type="Emphasized"
                                    class="sapUiMediumMarginTop"/>
                        </HBox>

                        <Table id="resultTable" items="{/submittedData}" width="100%" growing="true" growingThreshold="20">
                            <columns>
                                <Column><Text text="Name" /></Column>
                                <Column><Text text="Age" /></Column>
                                <Column><Text text="Company" /></Column>
                            </columns>
                            <items>
                                <ColumnListItem>
                                    <cells>
                                        <Text text="{name}" />
                                        <Text text="{age}" />
                                        <Text text="{company}" />
                                    </cells>
                                </ColumnListItem>
                            </items>
                        </Table>
                    </VBox>

                </VBox>
            </ScrollContainer>
        </Page>
    </App>
</mvc:View>
 
XML Namespaces (in SAPUI5, they are often referred to as "UI5 Namespaces").

In an XML-based SAPUI5 view (like View.xml), XML namespaces define which library or module each tag or control belongs to. They prevent naming conflicts and allow the use of multiple libraries in one file

 

  • xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    Enables use of MVC tags like <mvc:View> for defining views and controllers.

  • xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
    Provides access to core UI5 controls and features like event handling, formatting, etc.

  • xmlns="sap.m"
    Default namespace for mobile-ready UI controls like Button, Input, VBox, etc.

  • xmlns:viz="sap.viz.ui5.controls"
    Gives access to VizFrame chart controls for data visualization.

  • xmlns:viz.data="sap.viz.ui5.data"
    Used to define datasets, dimensions, and measures for VizFrame charts.

  • xmlns:viz.feeds="sap.viz.ui5.controls.common.feeds"
    Maps data to chart axes (e.g. valueAxis, categoryAxis) in VizFrame.

  • xmlns:export="sap.ui.export"
    Provides access to export utilities like Spreadsheet for downloading Excel files.

 

 

Brief Summary of the SAPUI5 View

This SAPUI5 XML View defines a simple Fiori-style web app UI that includes:

  • Header: Displays the title “Dummy App” at the top.

  • Form Section: Allows the user to enter Name, Age, and Company using input fields with value help dialogs (for selection support).

  • Chart: Shows a column chart that visualizes Age-wise count data, using sap.viz.ui5.controls.VizFrame.

  • Data Table: Displays submitted form entries (Name, Age, Company) in a growing table.

  • Buttons:

    • "Submit" to send form data.

    • "Download" to export the table (e.g., to Excel).

    • "Upload" to import data (e.g., from a file).

View1.controller.js:

 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageBox",
    "sap/viz/ui5/controls/VizFrame",
    "sap/viz/ui5/data/FlattenedDataset",
    "sap/viz/ui5/data/DimensionDefinition",
    "sap/viz/ui5/data/MeasureDefinition",
    "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel",
    "sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/m/SelectDialog",
    "sap/m/StandardListItem",
    "sap/ui/model/Filter",
    "sap/ui/model/FilterOperator"
], function (
    Controller,
    MessageBox,
    VizFrame,
    FlattenedDataset,
    DimensionDefinition,
    MeasureDefinition,
    JSONModel,
    Spreadsheet,
    MessageToast,
    SelectDialog,
    StandardListItem,
    Filter,
    FilterOperator
) {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("com.sap.btp.project1.controller.View1", {
        onInit: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();
    var oModel = new JSONModel({
        name: "",
        age: "",
        company: "",
        dropdownData: {
            names: [],
            ages: [],
            companies: []
        },
        submittedData: [],
        ageChartData: []
    });
    oView.setModel(oModel);

            var sCSVPath = jQuery.sap.getModulePath("com.sap.btp.project1", "/model/dropdown_master.csv");

            jQuery.ajax({
                url: sCSVPath,
                dataType: "text",
                success: function (data) {
                    var lines = data.split("\n").filter(Boolean);
                    var names = [], ages = [], companies = [];
                    var ageCountMap = {};

                    for (var i = 1; i < lines.length; i++) {
                        var values = lines[i].split(",");
                        if (values.length < 3) continue;

                        var nameVal = values[0].trim();
                        var ageVal = values[1].trim();
                        var companyVal = values[2].trim();

                        names.push({ key: nameVal, text: nameVal });
                        ages.push({ key: ageVal, text: ageVal });
                        companies.push({ key: companyVal, text: companyVal });

                        if (ageVal) {
                            ageCountMap[ageVal] = (ageCountMap[ageVal] || 0) + 1;
                        }
                    }

                    var ageChartData = Object.keys(ageCountMap).map(function (age) {
                        return {
                            age: age,
                            count: ageCountMap[age]
                        };
                    });

                    oModel.setProperty("/dropdownData/names", names);
                    oModel.setProperty("/dropdownData/ages", ages);
                    oModel.setProperty("/dropdownData/companies", companies);
                    oModel.setProperty("/ageChartData", ageChartData);
                },
                error: function () {
                    MessageToast.show("Failed to load dropdown CSV data.");
                }
            });
        },

        // Value Help for Name
        onNameValueHelp: function () {
            var oModel = this.getView().getModel();
            var aNames = oModel.getProperty("/dropdownData/names");

            var oDialog = new SelectDialog({
                title: "Select Name",
                items: aNames.map(function (item) {
                    return new StandardListItem({ title: item.text });
                }),
                liveChange: function (oEvent) {
                    var sValue = oEvent.getParameter("value");
                    var oFilter = new Filter("title", FilterOperator.Contains, sValue);
                    oDialog.getBinding("items").filter([oFilter]);
                },
                confirm: function (oEvent) {
                    var selected = oEvent.getParameter("selectedItem");
                    if (selected) {
                        this.getView().byId("nameInput").setValue(selected.getTitle());
                    }
                }.bind(this)
            });

            oDialog.open();
        },

        // Value Help for Age
        onAgeValueHelp: function () {
            var oModel = this.getView().getModel();
            var aAges = oModel.getProperty("/dropdownData/ages");

            var oDialog = new SelectDialog({
                title: "Select Age",
                items: aAges.map(function (item) {
                    return new StandardListItem({ title: item.text });
                }),
                liveChange: function (oEvent) {
                    var sValue = oEvent.getParameter("value");
                    var oFilter = new Filter("title", FilterOperator.Contains, sValue);
                    oDialog.getBinding("items").filter([oFilter]);
                },
                confirm: function (oEvent) {
                    var selected = oEvent.getParameter("selectedItem");
                    if (selected) {
                        this.getView().byId("ageInput").setValue(selected.getTitle());
                    }
                }.bind(this)
            });

            oDialog.open();
        },

        // Value Help for Company
        onCompanyValueHelp: function () {
            var oModel = this.getView().getModel();
            var aCompanies = oModel.getProperty("/dropdownData/companies");

            var oDialog = new SelectDialog({
                title: "Select Company",
                items: aCompanies.map(function (item) {
                    return new StandardListItem({ title: item.text });
                }),
                liveChange: function (oEvent) {
                    var sValue = oEvent.getParameter("value");
                    var oFilter = new Filter("title", FilterOperator.Contains, sValue);
                    oDialog.getBinding("items").filter([oFilter]);
                },
                confirm: function (oEvent) {
                    var selected = oEvent.getParameter("selectedItem");
                    if (selected) {
                        this.getView().byId("companyInput").setValue(selected.getTitle());
                    }
                }.bind(this)
            });

            oDialog.open();
        },

        onSubmit: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var name = oView.byId("nameInput").getValue();
            var age = oView.byId("ageInput").getValue();
            var company = oView.byId("companyInput").getValue();
       
            var oModel = oView.getModel();
           
            // Make sure these values are set to the model
            oModel.setProperty("/name", name);
            oModel.setProperty("/age", age);
            oModel.setProperty("/company", company);
       
            var validAges = oModel.getProperty("/dropdownData/ages").map(a => a.text);
            var validCompanies = oModel.getProperty("/dropdownData/companies").map(c => c.text);
       
            if (!age || !validAges.includes(age)) {
                MessageBox.error("Please select a valid Age.");
                return;
            }
            if (!company || !validCompanies.includes(company)) {
                MessageBox.error("Please select a valid Company.");
                return;
            }
       
            var message = `Name: ${name}\nAge: ${age}\nCompany: ${company}`;
            MessageBox.information(message, { title: "Submitted Details" });
        },
       

       
        onDownloadExcel: function () {
            var oModel = this.getView().getModel();
            var aData = oModel.getProperty("/submittedData");

            var oExportData = aData.map(function (item) {
                return {
                    "Name": item.name,
                    "Age": item.age,
                    "Company": item.company
                };
            });

            var oSpreadsheet = new Spreadsheet({
                workbook: {
                    columns: [
                        { label: 'Name', property: 'Name' },
                        { label: 'Age', property: 'Age' },
                        { label: 'Company', property: 'Company' }
                    ]
                },
                dataSource: oExportData,
                fileName: "table_data.xlsx"
            });

            oSpreadsheet.build().then(function () {
                MessageToast.show("Excel file has been downloaded!");
            });
        },

        onUploadFile: function () {
            var oInputFile = document.createElement("input");
            oInputFile.type = "file";
            oInputFile.accept = ".xlsx, .xls";
            oInputFile.onchange = this._onFileSelected.bind(this);
            oInputFile.click();
        },

        _onFileSelected: function (oEvent) {
            var oFile = oEvent.target.files[0];

            if (!oFile) {
                MessageToast.show("No file selected.");
                return;
            }

            var loadXLSX = function () {
                return new Promise(function (resolve, reject) {
                    if (typeof XLSX !== "undefined") {
                        resolve();
                        return;
                    }

                    var script = document.createElement("script");
                    script.src=jQuery.sap.getModulePath("com.sap.btp.project1") + "/libs/xlsx.full.min.js";
                    script.onload = resolve;
                    script.onerror = function () {
                        reject(new Error("Failed to load XLSX library."));
                    };
                    document.head.appendChild(script);
                });
            };

            loadXLSX().then(function () {
                var reader = new FileReader();

                reader.onload = function (e) {
                    try {
                        var data = new Uint8Array(e.target.result);
                        var workbook = XLSX.read(data, { type: 'array' });

                        var firstSheetName = workbook.SheetNames[0];
                        var worksheet = workbook.Sheets[firstSheetName];

                        var jsonData = XLSX.utils.sheet_to_json(worksheet);
                        var cleanedData = jsonData.map(function (item) {
                            return {
                                name: item.Name || "",
                                age: item.Age || "",
                                company: item.Company || ""
                            };
                        });

                        var oModel = this.getView().getModel();
                        oModel.setProperty("/submittedData", cleanedData);

                        MessageToast.show("Excel data loaded successfully.");
                    } catch (err) {
                        console.error("Excel parsing error:", err);
                        MessageToast.show("Failed to read Excel file.");
                    }
                }.bind(this);

                reader.readAsArrayBuffer(oFile);
            }.bind(this)).catch(function (err) {
                console.error(err);
                MessageToast.show("Could not load XLSX library.");
            });
        }
    });
});
 

SAPUI5 Module Imports & Their Purpose

"sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
Base class for all controllers. Used to define your custom controller logic.
"sap/m/MessageBox"
Used to show popup dialog boxes (e.g., for errors, confirmations, info messages).
"sap/viz/ui5/controls/VizFrame"
Core control for rendering charts (bar, pie, line, etc.) in SAPUI5 apps.
"sap/viz/ui5/data/FlattenedDataset"
🔹Defines the data format for charts, mapping flat JSON data to visual elements.
"sap/viz/ui5/data/DimensionDefinition"
Specifies dimensions (e.g., Age) used on the x-axis or category axis of the chart.
"sap/viz/ui5/data/MeasureDefinition"
Specifies measures (e.g., Count) used on the y-axis or value axis of the chart.
"sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel"
Provides a client-side data model using JSON, used to bind data to the UI.
"sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet"
Used for exporting table data to Excel (.xlsx) format.
"sap/m/MessageToast"
Displays temporary messages (toasts) at the bottom of the screen (e.g., “Excel downloaded!”).
"sap/m/SelectDialog"
A searchable dialog used in value help (dropdown) selections.
"sap/m/StandardListItem"
UI element representing a standard item in a list, used inside SelectDialog.
"sap/ui/model/Filter"
Enables filtering of items (e.g., in the value help dialog based on user input).
"sap/ui/model/FilterOperator"
Provides filter operations like Contains, EQ, etc., for filtering logic.
 

Key Functionalities Explained Briefly:

  1. onInit() – Load CSV Data:

    • Loads dropdown_master.csv from the model folder.

    • Extracts Name, Age, and Company options.

    • Stores the values in a JSON model under /dropdownData.

    • Also generates chart data (/ageChartData) based on age counts.

  2. Value Help (SelectDialog) for Each Field:

    • onNameValueHelp(), onAgeValueHelp(), onCompanyValueHelp() open a dialog with search.

    • Items are fetched from the loaded CSV data (/dropdownData).

    • The selected item is set in the respective input field.

  3. onSubmit() – Handle Form Submission:

    • Validates that Age and Company are from valid lists.

    • Shows a message box with the entered Name, Age, and Company.

  4. onDownloadExcel() – Export to Excel:

    • Downloads the /submittedData model (form entries) as an Excel .xlsx file.

  5. onUploadFile() – Upload Excel File:

    • Lets the user upload an Excel file.

    • Reads and parses it using the XLSX library.

    • Updates /submittedData in the model, populating the table in the UI.

 

 

The data shown in the value help comes from a CSV file named dropdown_master.csv, which is located in the model folder inside the webapp directory. This CSV file is loaded as a model and used to populate the dropdown options in the value help dialog.

 

 
In the app, when you click on the value help  icon, a popup dialog opens displaying a list of selectable options. These options—for Name, Age, and Company—are all loaded from a CSV file (dropdown_master.csv) located in the model folder. The same logic applies to each input field.
 
 
 

 

I’ve included a GIF in the demo to visually showcase how the app functions.

 

Learning Materials:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/develop-sapui5-applications 

Learning SAPUI5 for Beginners - SAP Community

Demo Kit - SAPUI5 SDK

SAP Tutorial Navigator | Tutorials for SAP Developer

entry.title: ✨ How to Identify SAP Fiori Apps Based on RAP (RESTful Application Programming Model)
entry.content:
📚Introduction

With the rise of SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP, the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) has become the gold standard for building cloud-ready, upgrade-safe applications. RAP brings together modern CDS views, OData V4 services, business object behavior definitions, and service bindings to simplify and accelerate application development.

As SAP increasingly delivers new Fiori apps using RAP—and updates existing ones into RAP-based versions—it's becoming critical for developers, architects, and even functional consultants to:

  • Identify which apps use RAP
  • Understand how to extend them using side-by-side or in-app extensibility
  • Recognize RAP-based capabilities like draft handling, validations, actions, and more

But here's the challenge: ❌SAP doesn't explicitly label Fiori apps as "RAP-based" in the Fiori Apps Library. So how can we reliably identify which apps are built using RAP?

This blog shows you how. Let's break it down with examples, tools, screenshots, and common pitfalls.

🔍Where to Begin? Fiori Apps Reference Library

Head to the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library. This is your primary tool for:

  • Searching apps by Line of Business (e.g., Finance, Procurement)
  • Filtering by SAP S/4HANA Version, UI Technology, and more
  • Getting details like OData service, App ID, and Extensibility options

⚠️But here's the catch: The library doesn't let you filter directly for "RAP-based apps". So you have to be smart about indirect clues.

 

 

 

 

 

⚡RAP Indicators in the Library

Apply these filters and clues:

  • UI Technology: Select SAP Fiori elements (RAP supports this by default)
  • Application Type: Select Transactional
  • OData Version: If available, filter or check for OData V4 (RAP requires this)
  • App Name or Version: Look for apps with names like:
    • "Manage Sales Quotation - Version 2"
    • "Manage Sales Contract - Version 2"

🔮Confirming RAP in the Backend (Step-by-Step)

If you've found a promising app in the Fiori Library, verify it in the backend using ADT (ABAP Development Tools) or SAP GUI.

✅Checklist: Is It RAP?

CheckpointRAP IndicatorTool

Service Binding exists

✅Required

ADT (Eclipse/BAS)

Service Definition used

✅Required

ADT

Behavior Definition present

✅Required

ADT

No SEGW project involved

✅Good sign

ADT or GUI

OData Version = 4.0

✅Strong hint

/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE

✅RAP-Based App Example

App ID F5630 (Manage Sales Quotation - Version 2):

  • OData = V4 ✅
  • Has Service Definition: UI_SALESQUOTATIONMANAGE✅
  • Has Service Binding: UI_SALESQUOTATIONMANAGE ✅
  • Has Behavior Definition: I_SalesQuotationTP ✅

🎉Conclusion: This is a RAP-based Fiori app.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

📊Diagram: How RAP-Based Fiori App Is Structured

UI_SALESQUOTATIONMANAGE (Service Binding, OData V4)

└── UI_SALESQUOTATIONMANAGE (Service Definition)

    └── [Projection CDS View – C_SalesQuotationManageTP]

        └── I_SalesQuotationTP (Root CDS View with Behavior)

            └── Behavior Definition (BDD)

✨Conclusion

Identifying whether a Fiori app is RAP-based takes a bit of detective work, but once you understand the key indicators—OData V4, service binding, behavior definition—you'll be able to:

  • Build smarter extensions
  • Create upgrade-stable extensions
  • Use modern features like drafts, validations, and late numbering

🚀Whether you're a developer or an architect planning your SAP S/4HANA roadmap, knowing how to filter and confirm RAP apps helps you take full advantage of the modern ABAP programming model.

If you found this blog helpful, share it with your team and Happy Learning Together !! 😉

entry.title: SAP Fiori Application Generator Available in Open Source
entry.content:
The SAP Fiori tools team is delighted to announce that the underlying code for the SAP Fiori Application Generator has been moved entirely to open source. This follows our vision that was first outlined by our Chief Architect in a blog post from 2021 (Yes, this has been a long journey ‌🙂‌). 

Why Open Source?

Our goals haven’t changed—only expanded: 

  • Tighter collaboration. Work side-by-side with the community rather than behind closed doors. 
  • Full transparency. See exactly how the generator works, suggest improvements, or fork it for your own needs. 
  • Faster innovation & broader adoption. Community pull-requests beat private roadmaps every time. 

Our open source GitHub project now contains multiple packages that comprise the SAP Fiori Application Generator and span functionality from generation to deployment, including but not limited to: 

PackageWhat it does
@sap-ux/axios-extensionHandles authenticated HTTP calls so your generator prompts fetch remote data seamlessly. 

@SAP-ux/*-writer

Reusable file writers that output consistent and version-appropriate SAP Fiori elements application files for building SAP Fiori Elements and freestyle SAPUI5 applications.
@sap-ux/deploy-toolingOpinionated deploy tasks for ABAP systems.
@sap-ux/odata-service-inquirer Provides reusable configurable inquirer prompts (for use in Yeoman generators) for connecting to multiple back-end types and retrieval of service data.
@sap-ux/fiori-app-sub-generator The main entry point for generation of SAP Fiori Elements based applications. Once a template type is selected in the @sap/generator-fiori generator, this is the sub-generator that co-ordinates prompting the user, using the inquirers and generates the files, using the writers.

You’ll also find shared utilities, end-to-end test suites, and plenty of docs. 

 Image - SAP Fiori Tools and Generators - High-Level Modules and Dependencies

Not a simple "Lift & Shift"

Before open-sourcing the code, we broke up a monolith, added TypeScript typings, improved test coverage, and simplified internal APIs so you get clean, reusable, and well-documented code from day one.

How Can You help?

• Utilize our tools, try generating a SAP Fiori app, deploy it, and tell us where we can improve?
• Star & fork the repos of interest to raise their visibility.
• Open issues or PRs—bug fixes, new features or documentation tweaks.

Is there anything missing that you think should be there? Let us know and open a feature request at https://github.com/SAP/open-ux-tools/issues.

entry.title: From idea to app: using AI-powered Project Accelerator to develop SAP Fiori apps
entry.content:
Why use the AI-powered Project Accelerator from SAP Fiori tools?

Imagine you're with colleagues or customers, standing at a whiteboard, sketching the UI of your future SAP Fiori elements app to determine what UI will meet your business requirements.

 

Or maybe you're designing the data model for that app:

 

 

What are the typical next steps to build a working SAP Fiori elements app?

You would normally:

  • Create a data model
  • Prepare the mock data
  • Build an OData service
  • Generate the SAP Fiori app
  • Add the necessary annotations

Now, with the AI-powered Project Accelerator from SAP Fiori tools, you can skip all those steps. Let the AI generate everything for you—including the data model, OData service, sample data, and UI annotations. How much time will that save you in your project? In this post, I’ll explain how to use this powerful new capability, including the steps to set up a trial account and try it for yourself.

Generating a prototype quickly is critical for gathering user feedback

At SAP, we frequently engage with customers in focused co-innovation events. Typically, these involve customers and SAP domain and product experts brainstorming solutions with designers. Then, a couple of dedicated developers invest hours (or even pull all-nighters) to create a prototype for next-day validation and refinement.

These bootcamps are incredibly productive, with every moment of collaboration being invaluable. The desire to expedite review and update cycles by seeing prototypes instantly has always been strong. Now, with the integration of AI technologies into our SAP Fiori tools, this instant prototyping is a reality—first for our internal development teams and now for all SAP customers seeking the same advantages.

In a few minutes, starting with a document, sketch, or other visual representation of your app, you can preview the UI of your list report – object page app.

List Report:

Object Page:

The generated project is fully compliant with SAP Fiori tools and can be deployed as a productive application when complete.

 

Ready to try out the AI-powered Project Accelerator?

 

Get started with either a paid SAP Build Code subscription or a free basic trial account.

Setup Steps with SAP Build Code subscription

You’ll need an SAP BTP account with an SAP Build Code subscription. Create a Full-Stack Dev Space containing both the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) and SAP Fiori tools extensions.

Setup Steps with a free basic trial.

Alternatively, use a free basic trial of SAP Build Code.
More details here:
👉SAP Trial Developer Tools

Note: the free basic trial may have limited quota for AI scenarios.

Click Try Now to generate your trial user ID and password.

Create Your First Project

  1. Log in to your SAP Build Code basic trial account.

  2. Open the Basic Trial – SAP Build Code Workbook.

  3. Follow the steps in Unit 2 – Create Project to create your Full-Stack Dev Space.

  4. At Step 6, personalize:

    • Project name (e.g., travels)

    • Description (e.g., Fiori Tools AI)

    • Dev space name (e.g., tryFioriToolsAI)

📌You do not need to complete any other chapters in the workbook.

Use the Project Accelerator

  1. In SAP Build Code, right-click in the left panel and select SAP Fiori.

  2. Open the Project Accelerator section.

The Project Accelerator is the AI component of SAP Fiori Tools that connects to a large language model (LLM) on SAP BTP. It automatically generates all artifacts for your application.

In the Application Requirements section, provide your business requirements.

Alternatively, you can use /fiori-gen-spec-app command in Joule. It will recognize your request and redirect you to the Project Accelerator:

 

Provide Your Business Requirements

As an input, you can use:

  • Text descriptions (Example 2: Display Customers with Related Contracts)

  • Images (Example: Manage Travel List Report Page). An image can represent a single page UI corresponding to the List Report Page or Object Page floorplan or a data model.

  • Combination of text and images (Example: Manage Travel App). If you want to add multiple images representing different app pages, you should use the combination of text and images, explaining in the text what the images referrer to.

Prepare user requirements as outlined above. For example, download an image of the data model from this blog, then:

  1. Open your workspace folder in SAP Build Code:

    • Go to File > Open Folder...

    • Select /home/user/projects/

  2. Create a folder under "Projects" (e.g., Requirements).

  3. Upload the image:

    • Right-click the folder and select Upload…

 

4. Switch to SAP Fiori from Explorer.

5. In Project Accelerator, click Choose File and select the uploaded image.

Then, click Generate.

 

Preview Your App

In a few minutes, the app will appear in the staging area with two options:

  • Accept Project

  • Preview

Feel free to review the code in the generated artefacts.

You can also select Preview to launch the CAP server and view the app in a new browser tab.

You’ll see an SAP Fiori elements app with:

  • A List Report (filter bar + table of travels)

  • A Travel Object Page

  • A Booking Detail Page (second object page)

 

 

If the generated app meets your needs, press Accept to move it to your dev space.

You can then use SAP Fiori tools to adjust your application UI. For more information on using SAP Fiori tools, see Getting Started with SAP Fiori tools  

Additional Resources

  • Supported Features in Application Requirements

  • Generating an App with the Project Accelerator or Joule

  • 🎥Watch the video: Project Accelerator: Elevate Development with AI in SAP Fiori tools

entry.title: Parallel universes: the dual nature of UX and AI among SAP customers at Sapphire 2025
entry.content:
Sapphire 2025 was interesting and revealing. In the keynotes, theater sessions, and ASUG breakouts, SAP experts and executives talked about the latest innovations around artificial intelligence (AI), data, and user experience (UX). The customer questions and survey responses, however, revealed a parallel universe. Many organizations are on such old systems that they are not able to consume SAP’s cool new functionality. In this post, I will discuss some of my observations, based on conversations with customers and partners, and share some polling I did in one of my sessions on the intersection between SAP Fiori and AI. I will also include links to several slide decks, recorded demos, and on-demand sessions where you can dive deeper into UX and AI topics.

Customers are starting to use AI, but still behind on SAP Fiori UX adoption

In ERP1612, “Enhancing User Experience with SAP Fiori and AI”, I asked the audience of around 120 people how they are using AI in their companies. 96 people responded.

 

The 56% percent of the people who are using AI in some way is a big jump from the 37% who responded at ASUG Tech Connect last fall. The main topic in both sessions was SAP Fiori, so the audience profile should be somewhat similar, although the Tech Connect attendees might be more technical as opposed to the business focus at Sapphire.

View the slides from ERP1612.

https://www.slideshare.net/slideshow/embed_code/key/1T0gHqRT6X9ddr?hostedIn=slideshare&page=upload

The central theme was SAP Fiori, so it made sense to level set on where people are on their adoption journey. My expectations were high since SAP Fiori has been available since 2013. Since then, our team has spent many hours talking to 1000s of customers and partners about building SAP Fiori apps and how to streamline the process using our tools and technologies such as SAP Fiori elements, SAP Fiori tools, and new AI accelerators.

But, the reality was very different. Only 9 of 109 respondents are using SAP Fiori across their organizations. 43% are still in the planning stage.

 

 

To help customers move to SAP Business Suite, we had many sessions. One that’s available on demand is “Embark on a successful transformation journey to SAP Business Suite,” featuring Maura Hameroff, Priyadarsana Panda, and Soeren Ruder.

Arpan Shah describes how SAP Business Suite drives transformational value through the suite’s strength and software-as-a-service qualities with integrated processes, data, and AI in the on-demand session “Leverage the benefits of SAP Business Suite in your cloud strategy.”

There were some nice demos of the latest SAP user experience in “SAP's user experience strategy to achieve better outcomes” by Arin Bhowmik and Emil Voutta.

Tom Reiss also had some great sessions on SAP Fiori innovations with nice demos, but unfortunately, they were not recorded.

Dr. Tom Reiss before his session.

AI in SAP Cloud ERP wowed the crowd

In Chris Hoffmann’s session, BAI1362, which I heard several people say was the best talk of the entire Sapphire, he showed several key innovations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Here are the slides and demos from his presentation:

  • Slides in PDF format
  • Insights card creation with Joule
  • AI-assisted easy filter
  • AI-assisted smart summarization
  • AI-assisted error explanation
  • AI-assisted smart solution for situations in My Home
  • SAP Fiori elements Project Accelerator

Chris Hoffmann during his talk about adding AI to SAP Fiori apps at both design time and runtime

Several executives from SAP and our customers discussed how AI is transforming business. See their session “Harness the benefits of data and AI in ERP and drive innovation” on demand.

For a bigger picture view on where these AI innovations fit into the broader SAP Business Suite, watch “Transform your enterprise with the power of SAP Business Suite.”

SAP Sapphire is a great place for discovery

The new (to SAP) harmonized product operating model focuses on discovery. Interacting with customers to understand their needs plays a big role for product managers and everyone involved in the development process. Sapphire and the after-hours events provide a great venue for conversations with customers about the problems they want to solve.

Since I’m known for my interest in user experience topics, most of my meetings and questions in my sessions focused on UX and adjacent topics. One of my goals was to gather feedback on what we show and address questions about what people need. While people seemed to like the new AI functionality in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that Chris and I showed in our respective sessions, the most common question was when it is coming to private cloud (planned) and on-prem (not planned).

There were several sessions on organizational change management, which seemed to be one of the main reasons that people have not migrated off the SAP GUI, even though they like to complain about it. Some of the main takeaways from the OCM panel were:

  • Prioritize people not technology
  • Change management takes a toll on people – prep for the after.
  • Don’t lose sight of the goal and decommission the old technology. Make sure to listen and be agile.
  • The journey will be difficult, acknowledge this and over-communicate.

I really enjoyed Paul Saunders’ entertaining and informative sessions on “Leveraging dark data for enhanced decision-making” and “Antifragile enterprises: How true resilience comes from embracing change.” These too were not recorded.

The panel on antifragile enterprises: How true resilience comes from embracing change

Too much content; too little time

Content overload was a common theme I heard from many of the people I spoke with at Sapphire. There were so many sessions crammed into the compressed 2-day format (compared to three days in years past).

BTP remained a hot topic, with many people looking to understand the best way to obtain value.

Discover SAP Business Technology Platform and its value for your business is a good overview on this.

Another good BTP session is “Unleash the power of your applications, data, and AI with SAP BTP.”

AI everywhere – separating reality from hype

AI was top of mind for everyone as it played such a major role in the keynotes. The most common issues that people raised with me were:

  • Concerns about privacy of their data
  • How much of the capability is real and can the results be trusted?
  • Availability of AI functionality outside of SAP Cloud ERP and the associated costs

SAP Sapphire is all about the people

It was great to meet several colleagues in person for the first time and to connect with those that I see only a few times each year.

If we did not meet at Sapphire and you are interested in joining the conversation about SAP Fiori application development and how AI makes it better, faster, and cheaper, please join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable group or sign up for our bi-monthly newsletter.

For the UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: SAP User Experience E-Learning: What's New and What's Next in SAP Fiori (Q1 2025)
entry.content:
For those of you involved with the SAP User Experience, especially SAP Fiori, you will be familiar with the insightful blogs of my colleagues, such as Thomas Reiss's article on the "SAP User Experience Q1/2025 Update – Part 1: Many New Innovations Available (AI, Joule and More)."

Building on these updates, we are excited to announce an E-Learning presented by the same author, Thomas Reiss. In this session, you will be guided through the available and planned user experience innovations, as well as the roadmap for 2025.

What to Expect in the E-Learning

Explore key highlights of recent and upcoming user experience innovations across SAP solutions:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Enhanced Homepage:

  • My Home: A personalized homepage with proactive notification of To-Do tasks and situation, insights, and direct access to applications.
  • SAP Start: Central entry point with task center, insights, and mobile access.

AI Innovations:

  • SAP Joule: Support Informational, navigational, and transactional interaction patterns, and insights cards creation on My Home.

Collaboration Enhancements:

  • Collaborative Draft with SAP Fiori elements v4 for selected applications in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Multiple users can edit business objects simultaneously.
  • SAP Collaboration Manager: In-app chat to avoid screen switching available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud.
  • Microsoft Teams Integration: Tasks and collaboration features now available for Public Edition 2502; and planned for Private Edition 2025.

SAP SuccessFactors

AI-Powered Experience:

  • AI-assisted features for onboarding, learning, career growth, and writing support.
  • Generative AI embedded into Microsoft Teams (e.g., interview question generation for hiring managers).

SAP Fieldglass

New Modern UI:

  • New home page that highlights what matters most to the users, with customizable content visibility for a personalized experience.

SAP Ariba

UX Enhancements:

  • The Initiatives Overview app simplifies category management with a clear, centralized view of all initiatives. It offers customizable filters, interactive charts, and a Gantt chart view to help you gain insights.

SAP Sales Cloud

Smart and Modern UI:

  • The Intelligent Sales Order revolutionizes the order-taking process with a seamless, intuitive interface designed for sales professionals.

SAP Build Work Zone / SAP Mobile Start / SAP Task Center

Integrated Digital Workplace:

  • SAP Build Work Zone: Central access with guided task completion using custom-defined wizards.
  • SAP Task Center: Open APIs for integration with non-SAP apps.
  • SAP Mobile Start: Supports favorites, situation handling via To-Dos, and foldable devices.

Before You Access the E-Learning

This E-Learning is brought to you by the SAP Enterprise Support Academy.

Important: To access the E-Learning, you must complete a one-time registration in SAP Learning Hub, edition for SAP Enterprise Support, using your S-User ID.
There is no additional cost involved.

📌Click here for the step-by-step registration guide

👉Once registered, you're ready to begin:

ACCESS NOW


Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

SAP Enterprise Support offers additional resources to help you drive innovation. SAP Enterprise Support value maps provide expert-led resources, tailored recommendations, and advisory services - all included in your SAP cloud subscription. Get started today: 

  • One-time sign up for SAP Learning Hub, edition for SAP Enterprise Support, using a valid S-User ID (please allow up to 2 hours for processing) 
  • Access SAP Enterprise Support value maps for personalized guidance 
  • Request a call with our solution area experts for tailored advice 

Value Maps are part of SAP Enterprise Support’s commitment to your success. Visit the SAP Enterprise Support value maps landing page to explore our range of topics and start engaging with us today.

Continue Reading

Discover more insights in the blog post series by Thomas Reiss:
