SAP User Experience Q1/2025 Update – Part 1: Many New Innovations Available (AI, Joule and More) ( (include the links to the other six posts related)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

About the author:

  • Carol Zhang is a Focus Advisor for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). Based in Singapore, she has been with SAP for over 20 years, holding various roles across the organization. She now helps businesses accelerate the adoption of SAP BTP tools and services through value map content and SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

Have questions, comments or feedback? Don't hesitate to comment down below or contact @CarolZhang.

entry.title: Mastering Complex SBPA Approvals : A Deep Dive into Wait Steps, Script Tasks, and API Integrations
entry.content:
Welcome back to our new blog series,  our previous blog series  delved into the intricacies of dynamic sequential approval workflows.

In this two-part series, my colleague @Adwait_Fadnavis  and I will explore some very critical business workflow  requirements  in detail using SAP Build Process Automation. 

Throughout this blog series, we will delve into the following critical business requirements:

Part 1 - Current Blog :Mastering Complex SBPA Approvals : A Deep Dive into Wait Steps, Script Tasks, and API Integrations

Part 2 : Demonstration with Complete end to end testing 

We will demonstrate how to use various SAP Build Process Automation tools to solve real-time challenges effectively.

This blog post addresses this complex requirement with detailed step-by-step instructions. You'll learn how to ensure workflows advance smoothly, even with  approval condition utilizing various innovative latest features such as: Wait Step with API Triggering , Workflow Context update via API etc.

Business Context and Problem Statement

While working on multiple SAP Build Process Automation projects, we have faced situations where workflows only proceed if all departments or approvers approve them unanimously.

If any approver rejects the proposal, the entire workflow is rejected . This creates a need for a parallel approval process, adhering to an "all-or-nothing" requirement, which SAP's standard solutions do not support.

In a below sample procurement workflow, the request is created and submitted. Both the Department Manager and Finance Department must approve it simultaneously. If either rejects, the workflow is terminated.

If both approve, the request goes to the Executive for final approval. The Executive's decision determines whether the request is processed or terminated.

Dynamic Number of Parallel Approval
Technical Solutioning & Design

In this process, we will create two workflows using SAP Build Process Automation’s Low Code/No Code approach.

Let’s refer to these as Workflow A (main workflow) and Workflow B.

Workflow A( Main Workflow): This is the main workflow, triggered with all input parameters, responsible for the overall business process flow.

Workflow B(Secondary Workflow): This workflow handles the approval task. It receives the Approval ID, total number of levels, and current level. If the final approval is granted, it triggers a wait step API  in Workflow A to proceed to the next level. If more approvals are needed, it sends the Approval ID to the next level.

 
Technical Design Steps for Workflow A:
 
  1. Trigger Workflow: The workflow is initiated with input parameters.
  2. Decision Creation: A decision step is created to determine the number of approvals require. 

3. Script Task: This step determines the total number of approvals and the current approval level.

$.context.custom.doneapprovalcount = 0;
$.context.custom.currentcount = 0;
$.context.custom.subwfdefinationid = "Your Secondaray Workflow ID"; // replace with your secondary workflow id
$.context.custom.totalapprovalcount = $.context.decision_getAppprovers_1.Approvers.length;
$.context.custom.triggeredsubprocessids = [];
​
  • Condition Check: If the current level matches the total required approvals, A wait step will be created to wait for the completion. If not, Workflow B is triggered to initiate the approval task.

  • Trigger Workflow B: Workflow B sends the work item to the respective user for approval.
  • Wait Step API : This critical step waits for all parallel approvals to be completed. Once done, the dummy decision is triggered by Workflow B. This is very interesting and latest features
    1. Create a Wait Step with API as follows. After Deployment it will event will create the API End Point which will used to trigger from secondary workflow B .      
    2. Execution ID is the Parent Workflow ID.
    3. Upload the API specification and create the action for this
    4.  Also make sure to create the API key which will be used to trigger it from secondary workflow B.

Technical Design Steps for Workflow B  (Triggered via Workflow A)

  1. Trigger from Workflow A : Workflow B starts when Workflow A reaches a certain point to manage approval tasks separately and in parallel to the main workflow.

  2. Send Approval Task: The approval task is sent to the current level approver to obtain necessary approvals at each level before proceeding.
  3. On Approval: The system retrieves instance details and updates the approval count to keep track of completed approvals and update the workflow status.  
  4. sample code
$.context.custom.approvedcount = $.context.action_get_getV1WorkflowInstancesWorkflowInstanceIdContext_6.result.custom.doneapprovalcount + 1;
  • Check Count: The system checks if all required approvals are obtained:
    • If all approvals are done, the workflow proceeds to the next step.
    • If not all approvals are done This ensures the workflow only proceeds once all approvals are obtained
  •  Update Parent Workflow A Approval Action : increment the update count. created a custom action. If all the approvals not done, then update the approval count via custom action. (refer our previous blog how to create action for workflow API)   
  • Retrieve Task ID & Trigger Wait API trigger wait step ( API) in Workflow A to signal that the approval process is complete / Reject  and it can proceed.
    • Now come to most crucial part of the Development: Trigger the wait step via API
    • Created a custom action project  for the wait step api end point.
    • Here is the Input mapping for the API call. Needs to provide the API Key and Parent ID.
    • It will trigger the wait step in workflow A to approve / move forward the workflow . 
 
In the upcoming blog post - Demonstration with complete end to end testing  , @Adwait_Fadnavis  will delve into the complete end to end testing for these which is very critical to understand the whole development flow.
 
Stay tuned for a comprehensive guide that will enhance your understanding and streamline your Development  process.

Regards,

Mohit Bansal,

SAP Mentor. SAP BTP Solution Architect

entry.title: Quickly Generate and Deploy SAP Fiori Apps from ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse
entry.content:
Last year, SAP Fiori tools added a closer integration with ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and enabled seamless navigation from ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse to SAP Fiori tools in both SAP Business Application Studio and Visual Studio Code. This enabled you to launch your configured external IDE environment and automatically start the SAP Fiori Application generator directly from the service binding editor in ADT. 

To increase your development productivity even more, this tool integration was now reused for the new Quick Fiori Application generator to create and deploy simple SAP Fiori applications. Like with SAP Fiori Tools in SAP Business Application Studio/Visual Studio Code, you as a developer are now able to generate an application based on SAP Fiori elements annotations and deploy the SAP Fiori UI effortlessly with only few clicks from the service binding editor in ADT. 

Want to know how?

  1. As a developer, create an OData UI Service based on the business object interface or CDS view suitable for UI consumption. OData UI services can be created in ABAP Development Tools for eclipse either manually by creating an OData UI service binding or by utilizing the Generate ABAP Repository Objects wizard. See Creating Service Binding and Generating ABAP Repository Objects.

  2. When creating the OData UI service in ABAP development tools for Eclipse, you must publish the corresponding service binding. For more information, see Using Service Binding Editor for OData V4 Service.

  3. In the service binding editor, select the entity set and choose Create Fiori App... 

     

  4. You now see two creation modes with which you can create SAP Fiori apps and deploy them to SAP BTP ABAP environment. Choose Create SAP Fiori app with Quick Fiori Application generator in ADT and choose Next.

     

  5. Enter the package name and configure the parameter values ensuring the repository name is unique and within 15 characters including namespace. Like in SAP Business Application Studio, the repository name corresponds to the name of the BSP application that gets generated post deployment. 

     

  6. Choose a transport request and choose Finish. The ABAP repository objects will be generated in the background, and you get a popup message once the objects are generated successfully. Choose Open to open or refresh the service binding.

  7. The BSP application and Fiori Launchpad App Descriptor item are generated in the chosen package along with two SICF objects. You now have a basic deployed Fiori app.

  8. In the service binding editor, you now see a clickable Fiori App URL. 


    Note, that the URL won't work for cloud systems immediately.     To consume services in an SAP Fiori app, you need to complete the following step.

  9. To allow business users to access the Fiori app, you must create an IAM app and link it to a business catalog. After that, your administrator can add your app to SAP Fiori Launchpad.
    Important: To make the app visible in the Fiori Launchpad in the end, the Fiori Launchpad App Descriptor Item that was generated after deployment needs to be added to the corresponding field in the IAM App. 


    This should automatically populate the OData service under the Services tab: 

That’s it! Easy, right? 

In the project explorer beneath your package, you now see the BSP application, the Launchpad App Descriptor item and the SICF objects along with the IAM app and the business catalog. 

To summarize, the new Quick Fiori Application generator – available as of SAP BTP ABAP environment 2505 and ABAP development tools for Eclipse client version 3.50 – provides you the ability to quickly generate a productive Fiori app for simple use case (CRUD on tabular data) and configure Fiori Launchpad settings all in one without a tool switch. 

However, the Quick Fiori App generator only supports list report floorplan-based Fiori apps. Besides that, UI development capabilities are limited to backend Fiori elements annotations. For any UI changes, or if you want to delete, redeploy or undeploy the application, you must generate a Fiori project from the deployed application subsequently. As mentioned in the beginning of this blog post, the creation of a Fiori project is also supported via the Create Fiori App… button in the Service Binding editor, from where you then can take over the project to SAP Business Application Studio/Visual Studio Code to perform further actions. Your Quick Fiori app will then be overwritten during deployment. 

We think, the Quick Fiori App generator is a powerful feature that enhances your productivity and enables you as a developer to deliver applications more rapidly and effectively. What do you think of this feature? Let us know in the comments!

entry.title: Support of SAP Build Work Zone, advanced and much more: SAP Mobile Start V2.2 & 2.3 - Release Update
entry.content:
In this blog, I will introduce the new features that we have released so far in 2025, with version V2.2 and our latest version V2.3.
When SAP Mobile Start got launched in August 2021, the app got automatically provisioned with SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition. In 2023, we added SAP Start and with the release V.2.2, SAP Mobile Start also connects to SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition.

When using SAP Mobile Start with SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition, you can now access your advanced edition content, like Workspaces or Feed Updates. As with the standard edition, your native and web apps are available within the Apps Screen of SAP Mobile Start, as well as in your Favorites Section on the Start Screen, based on your individual selection. This allows you direct and fast access to your business content while being mobile. 
What about the SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition tasks and notifications? If you have set up the Alert Notification Service and SAP Task Center Service, then you find them next to your business tasks and situations in the To-Do sections and in the Notification Center of SAP Mobile Start.

Screenshot: Notification Center with advanced edition notifications

We are looking forward to seeing many SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition and SAP SuccessFactors Work Zone customers to onboard to SAP Mobile Start, as it is automatically available if the SAP Mobile Start “toggle” is enabled in the Site Manager.

Remark: SAP Mobile Start is not the successor app for the SAP Build Work Zone Advanced mobile app. SAP Mobile Start focuses on providing users easy access to their applications and business workflows. The app is deeply integrated into the device OS and provides native push notifications and widgets to ensure a best-in-class user experience. The SAP Build Work Zone Advanced mobile app focuses more on the collaboration aspects. Users can use both apps in parallel. Please also see our respective knowledge base article for further details. 

Besides seeing your latest open To-Dos in SAP Mobile Start, you can now also see your completed To-Dos. Just tap the “Completed” filter button on the To-Do Screen and you will get your completed to-dos from the last six months right on the To-Dos Screen. You can also search across all open and completed to-dos via the search function.

Screenshot: Completed To-Dos

My favorite new feature is the site switch that is available with V2.3. This feature allows you to directly switch between SAP Build Work Zone sites. This is very helpful for IT teams but also for business users who work with multiple SAP Build Work Zone instances. Instead of always having to go through the complete onboarding process, the app offers you a list of recently used sites and you can just pick the one you need. Outdated sites can be deleted from your list with an easy swipe to the left. I am sure that you will love this feature.

Screenshots: Site Switch

Another nice enhancement comes for the news tiles. With the latest release of SAP Mobile Start, your news tile can also include news images. Furthermore, news feeds that require authentication are supported as well with the latest release. You just need to maintain the authenticated URL in the tile news property field “mobilestart.feedUrl” in the site manager. Please have a look at our SAP Help documentation for details.

Screenshots: News with Image

You can now see the source system name on the app tiles with the latest release, what is very helpful if you have for instance connected multiple SAP S/4HANA systems to SAP Build Work Zone. The system name helps you to easily identify the system that is behind the tile. To use this feature, you can enable it in the Site Manager (“Display: Show Source System Name on Tiles”).

Finally, the latest release provides some nice UX enhancements, like the enhanced navigation bar for Apple iPad and a new fresher design for monitoring details on Apple Watch. When you onboard to the app for the first time, a new in-app guide introduces the app features to you. In the Profile and Settings screen, you can now find detailed information about language settings for the app and for the displayed content; furthermore the app is now available in Hungarian.

Screenshot: New watch design 

I hope you will enjoy those new features in SAP Mobile Start.

For further information on the new topics, please check our SAP Mobile Start documentation.

SAP Mobile Experience offers intelligent native mobile solutions that help businesses build more efficient, resilient and sustainable end-to-end processes, improving people’s work life wherever they are.

Visit SAP Mobile Experience Community Page and click “follow” to get the latest development and innovation of our solutions. We look forward to hearing about your experience with setting up the solution in your landscape; please do share your thoughts and comments below. Enter here for additional questions regarding SAP Mobile Experience Applications.

entry.title: Unsafe-eval error in SAP Build Work Zone: Understanding and fixing a CSP blockage
entry.content:
While developing a SAP Fiori application in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), I embedded an external component within a custom SAPUI5 component. The application worked perfectly in the local development environment, but once deployed into SAP Build Work Zone (BWZ), it no longer loaded as expected.

The browser console revealed an error related to the Content Security Policy (CSP), specifically due to the indirect use of eval(). This blog post summarizes my learnings about CSP and how I resolved the issue.

What is the Content Security Policy (CSP)?

CSP is a web security standard defined by the W3C and enforced by web browsers. It allows developers to control which resources (scripts, images, styles, etc.) a browser is allowed to load and execute, helping to prevent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks and malicious script injections.

For example, the following directive means that only scripts from the same origin as the application are permitted.

Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self'

Other common directives include:

  • style-src: Specifies allowed sources for stylesheets.
  • img-src: Limits where images can be loaded from.
  • connect-src: Defines allowed sources for fetching resources like APIs.
  • default-src: Specifies a fallback for all resource types if not otherwise defined.
  • frame-ancestors: Controls which sources can embed the page in an iframe.
  • … 

To learn more about CSP, refer to the official documentation on MDN: https://developer.mozilla.org/fr/docs/Web/HTTP/CSP

Problem context

The application was developed in SAP Business Application Studio and contained an external web component wrapped inside a custom SAPUI5 component.

Once deployed to SAP Build Work Zone, the interface no longer displayed at all. The browser console then showed the following error:

Unsafe-eval error (CSP Compliant)
Uncaught (in promise) EvalError:
Refused to evaluate a string as JavaScript because 'unsafe-eval' is not an allowed source of script
in the following Content Security Policy directive: "script-src * data: blob:".

Error Meaning

In CSP, dynamic functions like eval() or new Function() are blocked by default because they can introduce serious security risks. These functions allow JavaScript code to be generated and executed at runtime, which can be exploited to inject and run malicious scripts—commonly associated with XSS attacks.

To protect the application's security, dynamic code execution is blocked by default in the Content Security Policy (CSP). Allowing it would require the inclusion of the unsafe-eval directive in the CSP, but this weakens security and should be avoided in production environments.

Root cause analysis

In the external component I used, I was generating an HTML element returned as a string (this is where I messed up). This HTML was then dynamically injected into the component’s DOM. However, the external component was interpreting this string using new Function(), violating CSP rules.

This behavior went unnoticed locally in Business Application Studio because CSP policies are often disabled or applied more leniently in development environments. However, in SAP Build Work Zone, a strict CSP is enforced, blocking dynamic code evaluation like this.

Implemented solution

By consulting the official documentation of the component, I found that I could provide a JavaScript function directly instead of passing a returned string containing HTML. This approach enables the component to use a safe rendering function, avoiding reliance on blocked mechanisms like eval() or new Function().

Here is an example of the new CSP-compliant code:

const generateDropdown = (aOptions) => {
  // Create the select element
  const oSelectElement = document.createElement('select');
  oSelectElement.classList.add('myCustomSelectClass');

  // Loop through the options array and create option elements
  aOptions.forEach(oOption => {
    const oOptionElement = document.createElement('option');
    oOptionElement.value = oOption.value;
    oOptionElement.textContent = oOption.label;

    // Append the option to the select
    oSelectElement.appendChild(oOptionElement); 
  });

  return oSelectElement;
};

// Example usage with an array of options
const options = [
  { value: '1', label: 'Option 1' },
  { value: '2', label: 'Option 2' },
  { value: '3', label: 'Option 3' }
];

// Generate the dropdown dynamically
generateDropdown(options);

Lessons learned

An application that works correctly in BAS may encounter critical errors in BWZ if it does not comply with the CSP. It is essential to prioritize the use of real JavaScript functions over injecting HTML or JavaScript code as strings. Often, the documentation for external components provides alternative solutions that are CSP-compliant, and these should be carefully considered.

Conclusion

The Content Security Policy is an essential tool for securing web applications against XSS attacks and the execution of uncontrolled code. In environments like SAP Build Work Zone, CSP is strictly enforced, which can lead to unexpected errors.

By adapting our implementation to comply with CSP rules, we not only ensure the compatibility of our applications but also enhance their security. I hope this feedback will be useful to you.

entry.title: $expand in UI5 SmartTable AnalyticalTable combo
entry.content:
Introduction

Hi and welcome to my next SAP Community blog post.

Even though, as of the time of writing this, the official UI5 SmartTable documentation does not say it, it is NOT possible to use $expand parameter with an AnalyticalTable used in SmartTable component. 

Actually, an example found in the SmartTable documentation might mislead into thinking, that it IS possible to do so. However, it is not... (https://sapui5.hana.ondemand.com/sdk/#/api/sap.ui.comp.smarttable.SmartTable%23faq)

In this blog, I will show you how to overcome this with a simple implementation of a custom JavaScript logic.

Root Cause

The root cause of the issue can be found in method _getQueryODataRequestOptions of class AnalyticalBinding. This is the binding used by the AnalyticalTable.

As you can see in the image pasted above, the $expand parameter is not taken into account when the query parameters are being parsed. 

Solution

A custom logic can be created to override this behavior. The logic should first copy the reference to the original logic (so that it can still be used) and add any additional logic we need.

For example:

// Override the original code of _getQueryODataRequestOptions method
// to allow $expand OData URL parameter for Analytical Table
_addExpandToAnalyticalBinding: function () {
  var that = this;
  this.fnGetQueryODataRequestOptionsOriginalLogic = sap.ui.model.analytics.AnalyticalBinding.prototype._getQueryODataRequestOptions;
  this.fnGetQueryODataRequestOptionsCustomLogic = function (oAnalyticalQueryRequest, bAddAdditionalSelects, mParameters) {
    var aResults = that.fnGetQueryODataRequestOptionsOriginalLogic.apply(this, arguments);

    // Add the needed $expand parameter values
    aResults.push("$expand=to_Items,to_Vendor");

    return aResults;
  };
}

 The _addExpandToAnalyticalBinding method can be called in the onInit handler of the view controller.

onInit: function () {
  this._addExpandToAnalyticalBinding();
}

And in the onBeforeRebindSmartTable event handler (for the "beforeRebindTable" event of a SmartTable control) the old logic should be overwritten with the new one.

onBeforeRebindSmartTable: function (oEvent) {
  // Override the original code of _getQueryODataRequestOptions method
  // to allow $expand OData URL parameter for Analytical Table
  sap.ui.model.analytics.AnalyticalBinding.prototype._getQueryODataRequestOptions = this.fnGetQueryODataRequestOptionsCustomLogic;
}

This way the standard logic will be appended with the needed $expand values.

Edit: I've noticed that there is an attributed in the AnalyticalBinding called sCustomParams. It could be used to add the $expand parameter to the OData call. Unfortunately, the AnalyticalBinding is not available when the AnalyticalTable is refreshed for the first time. The solution could be:

onBeforeRebindSmartTable: function (oEvent) {
  var oBinding = oEvent.getSource().getTable().getBinding();
  if (oBinding) {
    oBinding.sCustomParams = "$expand=to_Items,to_Vendor";
  }
  
  // Override the original code of _getQueryODataRequestOptions method
  // to allow $expand OData URL parameter for Analytical Table
  sap.ui.model.analytics.AnalyticalBinding.prototype._getQueryODataRequestOptions = this.fnGetQueryODataRequestOptionsCustomLogic;
}

The $expand string could also be moved to a separate method, common to both places

entry.title: Build and Deploy a Full-Stack SAP CAPM App with Fiori UI on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
Build and Deploy a Full-Stack SAP CAP Application with Fiori UI on BTP Cloud Foundry – A Complete Guide.

The Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model by SAP is a robust, opinionated framework that simplifies the creation of enterprise-grade services and applications that are scalable, extensible, and cloud-ready. In this in-depth guide, we walk you through every step of building a full-stack CAP project from the ground up using SAP Business Application Studio (BAS). You'll enhance it with a Fiori UI and deploy the entire application stack to SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) on Cloud Foundry. This guide is crafted with detailed, beginner-friendly explanations and valuable insights for intermediate developers aiming to master modern SAP application development.

 ==Prerequisites and Initial Setup==

 Before we begin, ensure you have an SAP BTP trial or productive account with the necessary entitlements, including SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition), and SAP Business Application Studio (BAS). These are essential for developing, deploying, and running CAP applications with Fiori UI on Cloud Foundry.

=== Creating a SAP HANA Cloud Instance===

To persist and manage data in your CAP application, you need to provision a SAP HANA Cloud instance in your BTP subaccount.

1.Log in to the SAP BTP Cockpit using your credentials.

2.Navigate into your global account and select the appropriate subaccount.

3.Under the navigation pane, click on "Service Marketplace" to view the available services.

4.Search for "SAP HANA Cloud" and click on it when it appears in the search results.

5.Click on the "Create" button to begin provisioning a new HANA database instance.

 

  1. Accessing SAP HANA Cloud Central
  2. Create New SAP HANA Cloud Instance

 

8.Sign-In with cloud foundry account and select your space from the dropdown

9.🔐 Administrator Section

During SAP HANA Cloud instance creation, you’ll need to configure administrative access:

  • Administrator Username: Pre-filled as DBADMIN (not editable)
  • Administrator Password: Enter a strong, secure password
  • Confirm Password: Re-enter the password to confirm

 

 

🔒Note: The DBADMIN password expires every 180 days. Be sure to set a reminder to update it before expiration to avoid access issues

 

10.Configure SAP HANA Database Settings

  • Available Memory Range: In the trial version, it's fixed at 16 GB.

11.SAP HANA Database Advanced Setting

🌐Network & Connectivity Settings

  • Allow All IP Addresses
  • Enable this option to allow unrestricted access to the database from any IP address.

🔗Enable Cloud Connector (Optional)

If your application or data sources require secure access to on-premise systems via the SAP Cloud Connector:

  • Toggle Enable Cloud Connector to ON
  • This allows secure integration between your SAP HANA Cloud instance and on-premise environments

 

12.Final Review and Create Instance

After configuring all the necessary settings — including instance name, memory size, admin credentials, network access, and optional Cloud Connector — take a moment to carefully:

 

  • Review the Administrator Username and Password
  • Verify Connectivity Settings (e.g., Allow All IP Addresses)
  • Confirm the Cloud Connector status (enabled or disabled)
  • Ensure there are no missing or incorrect entries

Once everything looks correct, click Create to start provisioning your SAP HANA Cloud instance.

 

⏳The process may take a few minutes. After successful creation, the status will change to Running — your database is now ready for use!

 

 

  1. Steps to Manage Configuration and Instance Mapping in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Click “Manage Configuration”:
  • Go to “Instance Mapping”:
  • Provide Cloud Foundry Environment Details:
  • Set the Environment to: Cloud Foundry
  • Click review and save

 

== 1. Creating a Dev Space in SAP Business Application Studio ==

  1. Open your browser and navigate to the SAP Business Application Studio.
  2. On the landing page, click on the "Create Dev Space" button, which will prompt you to select a template.
  3. From the available options, choose "Full-Stack Cloud Application" as it comes pre-configured with essential tools for CAP and Fiori development.
  4. Assign a recognizable name to your dev space, for example, capm-book-app, to easily identify it later.
  5. Check the box labeled "SAP HANA Tools" to enable support for creating and managing HANA database artifacts.
  6. Click on "Create Dev Space" and wait patiently for the status of the space to change to "RUNNING."
  7. Once the dev space is up and running, click on it to launch the integrated development environment where you will build your application.

 

2.Step-by-Step: Creating a New CAP Project from Template

  • Open SAP Business Application Studio (BAS)
  • Log in to your trial or productive BAS workspace.

2.1 Open the Command Palette

  • Click on the View menu → Find Command
  • Or press F1 or Ctrl + Shift + P to open the Command Palette

2.3 Choose   New   Project   from Template

  • From the list, choose:  "CAP Project" (Cloud Application Programming Model)
  • Click Next

2.3 Fill Project Details

  • Project Name: e.g., bookshop
  • Choose Runtime: Node.js
  • Choose  Add  Samples   Yes (optional, useful for beginners)
  • Click  Finish

 

 

2.4 . Open in Workspace

  • If prompted: Click "Open in New Workspace"
  • The wizard will scaffold a full CAP project structure with:

== Modeling Data and Defining Services ==

  1. Define the Data Model

Create a new CDS file in the db folder, typically named data-model.cds:

 3.1 Add Sample Data in CSV Format

Create a new CSV file inside the db/data directory named my.bookshop-Books.csv:

   ID,title,stock,author

  1,The Pragmatic Programmer,42,Andrew Hunt

  2,Clean Code,37,Robert C. Martin

  3,Domain-Driven Design,15,Eric Evans

 4,Introduction to Algorithms,21,Thomas H. Cormen

 5,Design Patterns,18,Erich Gamma

 6,Refactoring,25,Martin Fowler

7,The Clean Coder,30,Robert C. Martin

8,Head First Design Patterns,40,Eric Freeman

9,Working Effectively with Legacy Code,10,Michael Feathers

10,Continuous Delivery,12,Jez Humble

                                                                  

3.2 Define a CAP Service

Inside the  srv  folder, create a service definition file named catalog-service.cds:

3.3 @odata.draft.enabled – CAPM Annotation:

  • The @odata.draft.enabled annotation enables draft handling for an entity in SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model). It supports Fiori Elements UIs by allowing users to create, update, save drafts, and finalize records without writing custom CRUD logic.
  • Adds special fields: IsActiveEntity, HasDraftEntity, HasActiveEntity.
  •  

  ⚠️ Notes:

·       Only works with OData V4.

 

 

3.4 Steps to Add a Fiori App Using SAP Fiori Generator:

  • Click File → New Project from Template
  • Select “SAP Fiori Application” and click Start
  •  Select “SAP Fiori Elements”

3.5 selecting the List Report Page template is a great choice for generating a powerful UI automatically from your CAP service.

  • The List Report Page is part of Fiori Elements, used to create a master-detail UI where

 

3.6 Choose Data Source and service selection :

  • If your CAP project is open in the same workspace
  • Use a local CAP project
  • Select Main Service Entity

 

3.7 After UI setup is finished:

Install dependencies
In your project root directory, run:

  •   npm install

 3.8 Start the CAP server with watch mode
Run:

  •     cds watch
  •   Starts your CAP service locally
  •  Automatically reloads when you make changes to .Cds, .js, or .json files

== 4. Configuring for SAP HANA and cloud foundary Deployment ==

CAP defaults to SQLite for local development, but to deploy on SAP BTP, you must configure it to use SAP HANA.

4.1  Add HANA configuration for production:

4.2  Adds XSUAA (SAP Authorization and Trust Management) to your project

  • Adding oauth2-configuration to xs-security.json for Local & CF Integration
  • Purpose Defines allowed callback URLs for authentication Allows redirect after deployment to Cloud Foundry (US10 region).

4.3 Add HTML5 Repo to Your Project:

  1. Adds configuration to support deploying UI modules (React, Fiori

  1. Updates the mta.yaml file to include:
  • html5-repo-host
  • html5-repo-runtime

 Enables deployment to SAP Launchpad / Work Zone or directly via managed app router

4.4 Adds an App Router to your CAP project for production deployment on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry.

1. The Application Router (Approuter) is a Node.js-based component that.

2.Routes requests to your CAP services (srv) and HTML5 apps (app).

3.Once deployed, the App Router becomes the single entry point URL for your entire application.

4.5 Adds mta.yaml file to your CAP project to enable deployment to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry using the Multi-Target Application (MTA) approach.

  • Creates the deployment descriptor required by Cloud Foundry
  • Combines your DB, service, UI, XSUAA, and HTML5 repo into one .mtar file
  • Manages Dependencies Links services like HANA, XSUAA, Approuter, and HTML5 apps together in one file.

  • Modules — what your app is made of
  • These are the individual deployable components (e.g., backend, UI, approuter).
  • Resources — external services your app uses
  • These are external backing services or dependencies your modules need.

4.6 Add workzone-standard  Launch your CAP app via a tile on SAP Work Zone standard edition

  • Adds a Work Zone module to your mta.yaml
  • This prepares the app for deployment to Work Zone.
  • Generates a manifest.json for Work Zone
  • Used to register the application (title, URL, etc.).

 

4.7 Install all necessary project dependencies:

4.8 Add Multi-Target Application Builder It comes from the SAP MTA SDK and builds all modules and combines them into a single .mtar file.

4.9 Deploy MTAR Archive to Cloud Foundry

  • The "Build and Deploy Completed" pop-up message appears after you build and deploy your CAPM or MTA project using SAP Business Application Studio or the CLI.

4.10 After deploying your MTA (.mtar) to Cloud Foundry, the application URL(s) become available in the Cloud Foundry space where you deployed.

 

 

Navigate to Your Destination in BTP Cockpit

  • View Your Configured Destinations
  • Test the destination (click on it → press "Check Connection")
  • Edit credentials, token URL, client ID/secret

4.11 After deploying an HTML5 application (like a Fiori or React app) to SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry) using html5-repo, the application URL becomes available

 

4.12 Access Your Deployed HDI Container in HANA Database Explorer:

  • In HANA Cloud Central, select your HANA DB instance
  • Click "Open in SAP HANA Database Explorer"
  • Open Database Explorer Add and open your HDI container Explore your tables and data.
  •  Add Your HDI Container
  • In Database Explorer, click the + icon in the left panel (next to “Filter Instances”)
  • Select:
  • Your Subaccount
  • Cloud Foundry Space
  • Your deployed HDI container (e.g., bookshop_app-db_hdi-container)
  • Click OK
  • Now your HDI container will appear in the left sidebar.

 

 

  • Open and Explore Tables
  • Click on "Tables"
  • Locate your table, e.g., MY_BOOKSHOP_BOOKS
  •  Right-click or double-click the table → Open Data

 

Conclusion:

   You now have a fully working SAP BTP full-stack application using CAPM + Fiori UI deployed on Cloud Foundry.

   Feel free to comment or reach out if you need help or enhancements.

entry.title: How to Embed a PowerBI Report within a Custom Fiori UI5 app
entry.content:
Hi Folks, 

In this post I'll go over a simple yet powerful requirement: embedding a PowerBI report in a custom SAP UI5 Fiori application. This can be a great way to enrich your Fiori applications with rich, interactive analytics. Utilize this to create a seamless, unified user experience which puts data-driven insights right where decisions are made.

Prerequisites

  1. A Power BI report that you are ready to share.
  2. Access to the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and the Business Application Studio (BAS).

 

Step 1: Get the Public Embed Code from Power BI

  1. Generate a link for our Power BI report. This is the simplest method for embedding.
  2. Follow the steps from the Microsoft knowledge base on how to embed Power BI reports into portals (also see screenshot)

 

Step 2: Create a new Fiori app in Business Application Studio

  1. Open Business Application Studio and start a new project from template
  2. Select “Sap Fiori Generator”
  3. Select “Basic”
  4. Select “None” for Data Source. We don't need to connect to an OData service for this example.
  5. Input a View Name followed by Project Attributes and click “Finish”

 

Step 3: Add the Code for embedding to your XML view

  1. Navigate to your project’s webapp > view folder and open the xml view you created.  
  2. Replace the existing code and insert the following code snippet in your home page view xml.
  3. Modify the controllerName, and from the content modify the frame title, width, height, src, and frameborder as necessary.
<mvc:View
    controllerName="com.mycompany.powerbiapp.controller.Home"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
    <Page id="page" title="INPUT_A_TITLE_HERE">
        <content>
            <core:HTML
                id="powerBIFrame"
                content='<iframe title="INPUT_A_TITLE_HERE " width="100%"
                height="100%" src=" INSERT_YOUR_POWER_BI_EMBED_URL_HERE "
                frameborder="0" allowFullScreen="true"></iframe>'>
            </core:HTML>
        </content>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>
  • Notice the < and >
  • ^ This is crucial! In XML views, you must escape the < and > characters with their HTML entity equivalents (< and >) to ensure the XML is valid.

(Example from my project)

 

Step 4: Preview your application

  1. Once your project is modified, save your project and preview the application by right clicking on the webapp folder and select “preview application.”
  2.  Choose one of the available preview scripts that shows up in the command palette (e.g., start-local).

Your Fiori application will launch in a new browser tab and you should see your PowerBI report embedded within your application

(Example from my project)

 

I hope you found this guide useful. Feel free to leave your questions and feedback in the comments below.

entry.title: SAP Fiori Innovation Day Bangalore -- Connection, Gratitude, AI, and Local Engineering Pride
entry.content:
The first-ever SAP Fiori Innovation Day in Bangalore was a tremendous success, bringing together 200+ participants for what became the largest SAP Fiori Innovation Day yet. This event provided local developers an opportunity to connect with customers, showcase our latest AI-powered innovations, and help us understand where to focus our development efforts.

In this post, I'll discuss the key sessions, share insights from our presentations, and highlight the remarkable contributions from our local development community.

Engineering Pride from Our Local Contributions to the UX Foundation Portfolio

We started with Ioannis Grammatikakis, SVP for UX Foundation at SAP, providing our UX strategy overview for SAP S/4HANA Cloud. His vision about intelligent UX and the humanness aspect that creates customer loyalty resonated strongly with our audience.

Ioannis Grammatikakis, SVP for UX Foundation at SAP, presenting the UX strategy and vision for SAP S/4HANA

We then demonstrated the latest AI functionality embedded across the SAP S/4HANA portfolio:

  • My Home: AI-powered app recommendations, intelligent insights cards, and Joule integration for personalized experiences
  • List Report: Natural language filtering that translates user requests into precise filter criteria
  • Object Page: AI summarization features allowing users to select sections and share results with colleagues

It was gratifying to see our local Bangalore team members demonstrating these features they directly contributed to developing. The audience engagement was particularly strong, with participants asking about integrating S/4HANA systems with Microsoft Teams services and bot platforms.

Hands-on Experience with SAP Fiori Tools

Our hands-on sessions led by Gaurav Rawat showed how SAP Fiori tools in Business Application Studio (BAS) addresses 80% of common use cases. Participants experienced the complete development cycle from template selection through deployment, with tools automatically handling metadata, annotations, and dependencies. The interactive nature of the sessions sparked excellent questions from participants, including how to configure simple filters in SAPUI5 Freestyle applications, setting up SAP Fiori tools extensions in BAS, and whether SAP Fiori apps developed through SAP Fiori tools can be containerized using Docker.

Participants working with SAP Fiori tools and exploring AI-powered development features

Interactive Demo Booths: Hands-on Exploration

Beyond the main stage presentations, participants had the opportunity to explore multiple demo booths throughout the day, each focusing on different aspects of SAP Fiori development and user experience:

Leverage SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library: Participants explored how to identify apps strategic for their business needs by leveraging the comprehensive library of available SAP Fiori applications and learning about installation options.

Intelligent Entry Experience to SAP S/4HANA: Demonstrations showcased the journey from first steps to daily excellence, featuring the latest My Home page in SAP S/4HANA systems, which serves as the standard entry page providing a personalized and unified user experience.

Seamless and Collaborative Experience with Technology-Agnostic Cards: This session focused on creating seamless user interactions through technology-agnostic cards that enhance collaboration and overall application usability.

AI-Assisted Easy Filter: A deep dive into redefining the filtering experience through innovation, featuring natural language filtering capabilities and AI-powered search functionality.

SAP Fiori Elements Fast-Track App Development: Best practices and strategies for boosting developer efficiency and building applications that can grow with business needs using SAP Fiori elements. Participants were particularly interested in the availability of public documentation for these app generation capabilities.

Interactive Demo Booths

Mindset's AI-Powered Innovation: Reimagining SAP Fiori User Experience

Our partner Mindset Consulting presented their session "Reimagining SAP Fiori: AI-Powered User Experience" showcasing their innovative document summary generator using LLM models. Their solution demonstrates how AI can automatically process and summarize documents, with the generated summaries being stored in HANA DB for efficient retrieval and reuse. This demonstration highlighted how partner solutions can extend SAP Fiori applications with advanced AI capabilities, showing practical applications of intelligent document processing in enterprise scenarios.

Developing Applications is a Team Sport, So Connections Matter

Modern enterprise applications require collaboration between business owners, user researchers, designers, enterprise architects, and developers—with AI tools increasingly becoming team members. The diverse skills of our Bangalore participants perfectly matched today's fusion development teams. The energy and engagement throughout the event will directly influence our future innovations.

SAP Fiori Innovation Day team

Gratitude for Making SAP Fiori Innovation Day a Huge Success

This event's success came from the energy and engagement of 200+ participants. Their questions, comments, and feedback provide invaluable guidance for future innovations.

Thanks to all SAP presenters for their valuable contributions. Special thanks to Mindset Consulting for sponsoring and their presentation on Reimagining SAP Fiori User Experience, and the SAP Inside Track team for integrating our UX track into their larger community event.

Watch for SAP Fiori Innovation Day in Your Region

  • Dallas, Texas, USA -- October 17, 2025 as part of the ASUG chapter meeting
  • Paris, France -- December 10, 2025

Continue Your Learning

  • Subscribe to the bi-monthly SAP Fiori Development Newsletter
  • Sign up for the SAP S/4HANA UX Roundtable in India (starting in summer 2025)
  • Join the monthly SAP Fiori Development Roundtable for U.S. and Europe if that fits your schedule better

entry.title: I Killed Fiori (And I'd Do It Again)
entry.content:
Or: How TRIZ Taught Me The Best UI Is No UI

 Hey SAP folks! 👋

 

Deep breath.

I have a confession: I killed Fiori. Not in one system — in all of them. Before you grab your pitchforks, hear me out.

I loved Fiori when it launched in 2013. Finally, SAP that didn't look like Windows 95! Responsive design! Tiles! But it's 2025, and we're still... clicking... through... tiles... to... do... everything.

After working with Fiori since 2015, I had an epiphany that would make any TRIZ (innovation theory) person weep with joy:

"The ideal system is one that doesn't exist but its function is performed."

The "This Can't Be The Future" Moment

Picture this: 9 AM Monday. CFO pings you: "Need the latest P&L for company 1000 — compare this quarter to last, right now."

The Fiori Way™:

  • Open Fiori Launchpad (wait for tiles to load)
  • Find "Financial Statement Analysis" app
  • Click tile (wait for app to load)
  • Select company code from dropdown
  • Choose fiscal period and year
  • Apply profit center filters
  • Navigate through multiple screens
  • Export to Excel for actual analysis

Time: ~4 minutes · 23 clicks · 8 screens · Frustration: Daily

 

The 2025 Way:

Me: Show me P&L breakdown for company code 1000, 
    this quarter vs last quarter

Claude: Q4 2024 vs Q3 2024 analysis ready:
        Revenue: +12% ($2.4M → $2.7M)
        EBITDA margin improved 3.2% 
        (cost optimization in logistics)
        Key driver: 18% increase in Product Line A

Time: 8 seconds · Clicks: 0 · Sanity: Preserved

How I Built The Ultimate Weapon

I knew exactly what I wanted to track. After years with Fiori, I understood the game. So I built a Chrome extension that captures:

  • Every click, input, form submission
  • Every OData request triggered by those clicks
  • Complete UI5 context and metadata
  • Atomic action decomposition with parametrization

The pattern was always there:

   User Action → UI Update → OData Call → Backend Logic

The TRIZ insight: Why do we need the first two steps at all?

   OData Call → Backend Logic

   (Whisper: MCP - OData Bridge!)

The Shocking Truth: 80% Pure Waste

My extension analysed hundreds of Fiori sessions across different apps. The results?

Real data from "Manage Detection Methods":

  • Duration: 27.7 seconds
  • Clicks: 10
  • OData requests: 15
  • @uiannotations involved: 23
  • Actual business logic: Update one field

Pattern discovery:

  • UI Layer: 80% overhead (pure translation)
  • Business Logic: 20% actual work
  • Innovation opportunity: Eliminate the 80%

Liberation from @ui Annotation Hell

Remember wrestling with cryptic @ui annotations? Hours spent debugging why fields don't appear, fighting responsive design, calling consultants when layouts break.

When you eliminate the UI, you eliminate the entire annotation ecosystem. Clean CDS views with pure business logic. No more @ui.* black magic.

The Three Pillars That Killed Fiori

  1. Parametrized Automation→ Record once, replay forever with different parameters
  2. Intelligent Test Generation → Describe in English, auto-generate reliable tests
  3. AI-Ready Business Tools → Convert interactions to semantic tools Claude understands

 

The Transformation Results

95% time reduction. 100% complexity elimination.

The "But What About..." Section

"But what about data visualization?" Great question! Complex charts and graphs? Keep those Fiori apps. But for the 80% of interactions that are just CRUD operations? Dead.

"But what about user permissions?" The OData services already handle that. If you can't do something in Fiori, the AI can't do it either. Security preserved, clicking eliminated.

"But what about complex workflows?" You mean like "Create PO → Route for approval → Update inventory"? Watch this:

"But what about mobile?" You know what works great on mobile? Chat. You know what doesn't? Trying to click tiny Fiori tiles on a phone screen.

What This Means For You

  • For Functional Consultants: Demonstrate a process once, get automation forever. No coding required.
  • For Developers: Focus on business logic. Let AI handle the interaction layer.
  • For Managers: Measure ROI in seconds saved × transactions per day. The math is beautiful.
  • For Everyone: The future isn't better UIs — it's no UIs at all.

Your Turn
